scored.feedTitle: The 405 Coffee Break with O.K. Solberg
scored.description:
Greetings and Welcome to The 405 Coffee Break with O.K. Solberg

New episodes tend to air over the local KMMR radio station @ 5 minutes past 4PM each M-F. And have been doing so, nearly every week since Sept 2018.

I'm D.J. Rasmussen, O.K.s friend since junior high, possibly your neighbor & this websites maintainer, whom strives to get each episode's show notes written, the transcript proofed and the audio posted to the internet within a few hours of that days KMMR air time. NOTE: recently been publishing most new releases by 4:30PM.

Thanks for visiting and I hope you enjoy the time we can spend together.

latestEpisode.title: The 405 Aired June 30th, 2026

Let's see what's happening. Spring wheat $5.63 a bushel. 550lb steer calf $5.56 a pound on the top end, and a 100lb fat lamb in Billings $3.33 a pound. But, guys, there's more, much more. Okay. Okay. But to start with, I will find us a bible verse to set the stage. Now it comes from the Old Testament, Old Testament book of Joel. We don't hear from that one very often. Old Testament book of Joel chapter 3 verse 9. Proclaim this among the nations. Consecrate for war. Stir up the mighty men. Let all the men of war draw near. Let them come up. Again, Joel 3:9. So our Bible verse and our US history will be tied to World War II, and that verse let all the men of war draw near. Well, guys, that was the battle cry in The US after the Japanese bombed Pearl Harbor on 12/07/1941. Now did you know there were things rationed during World War II? In fact, I'm too young to have seen it firsthand, but I can guarantee it was a real thing because I have two booklets of ration coupons from the 1940's in my office. I like that kind of stuff. A keepsake, a relic, a reminder of how things used to be. So as you probably know, coffee, gasoline, and sugar were rationed. America needed to save as much as we could for our fighting people across the ocean. Well, sugar, coffee, and gasoline weren't the only things rationed. Copper was in high demand. So what happened in 1943? You know, pennies were made with copper back then. In 1943, they stopped making the traditional penny, and they used steel coated with zinc. The zinc made them have a silver gleam to them. In fact, in that year, they saved enough copper to manufacture 1,250,000 shells of ammunition. Now with the stage being set for US history, let me now pull in local history. I know a person who was a young girl in Phillips County back then, and her brother and her drove to Malta to pick up a sister who was coming from the East on a train. Well, they got to Malta early, and the brother, well, he liked to play cards at the Pastime bar. But he wasn't gonna take his 13 year old sister into the bar. No. It can't do that. So he reached in his pocket and pulled out a dime and told his younger sister she could go to the movie over there at the Palace Theater. Well, she got to the Palace Theater right here in Malta, Montana, and she handed the ticket taker her dime. Because, guys, remember back then, it cost only a dime to go to the movie. What a deal. Well, the ticket taker who happened to be Billy Jim Campbell's younger brother, Josh Paul Campbell, he looked at the coin the young girl handed him and he said, this isn't a dime. It's a penny. Well, the young girl was brokenhearted. She clutched the silver coin in her hand, walked back to the car that was sitting outside the Pastime bar, and waited till her brother came out. It was a long wait and no movie today, all because a penny looked like a dime. Now, guys, I just learned that story today, and I couldn't wait to share it with you. In 1944, the US Mint went back to making pennies out of copper. What was their reason? I'll tell you. They went back because they rusted, they confused vending machines, and they were frequently mistaken for a dime as is the story of the young girl's case at the Palace Theater. That's just a good story. I don't care who you are. That's just fun. But it also happened to happen in good old Malta, Montana. Now, guys, I have 3 Palace Theater stories. Pat Murdock, Jimmy's mom, remembers that the Saturday matinee cost only 9¢. Oh, yes. They enticed more business on Saturday afternoon by dropping the price by 1p. Then, of course, you remember Gary Fado's story. He was outside the Palace Theater as a young boy, and he had a dime in his hand, and it was a real dime. It was winter. It was cold. And he dropped his dime in the snow, and he looked and he looked and he looked, but no success. And that brought the young lad to tears. But a kind lady saw him crying and asked him what the problem was. He told her she opened her purse and gave him a dime, a real dime. Nice lady, good story, but it didn't end there. Gary was a thinker, so he thought it over and he realized if he had dropped a dime, other kids might have dropped their coins as well. So he knew where the city piled the snow when they removed it from the streets and sidewalks. He waited till spring, the snow melted, and he took a careful search and ended up walking home with a dollar and 22¢ that day. That there is a money making deal. It's fun to have fun. If you have a Palace Theater story, you make sure you let me know. I love old history. So until next time, as you go out there, remember now, don't be bitter.

relevance.rationale: The episode is a nostalgic local history segment about WWII-era rationing and small-town Montana memories, with no connection to current news or political events.

clipability.rationale: The Gary Fado snow-and-coins story and the 1943 steel penny anecdote are genuinely self-contained, charming vignettes that could stand alone as 30-45 second previews without much setup.

card.tagline: A steel penny, a dime mix-up, and a Montana movie theater that cost 9 cents
card.description: O.K. Solberg traces WWII copper rationing to a heartbreaking Palace Theater moment in Malta, Montana — and a kid who got rich waiting for the snow to melt.
card.reason: Because this episode tells a forgotten small-town story rooted in American wartime history

scored.description: TribCast is a weekly political podcast hosted by Matthew Watkins and Eleanor Klibanoff, and featuring a rotating cast of editors, reporters and other guests. The TribCast is returning as a limited-run weekly series during the 2025 legislative session.

scored.description:
The Diddy Diaries: The Downfall of Sean Combs is a raw, unflinching look at the dramatic collapse of one of hip-hop’s biggest moguls. For decades, Sean 'Diddy' Combs seemed untouchable—a figure who rose from the streets to become a titan of the music, fashion, and entertainment industries. His Bad Boy Entertainment label defined a generation of hip-hop, and his entrepreneurial spirit made him a household name. But beneath the surface of this glittering success lay darker currents—currents that have now surged into the spotlight as the empire he built threatens to crumble beneath the weight of scandal and serious criminal allegations.

This podcast dives deep into the allegations that have sent shockwaves through the entertainment world. At the center of it all are the accusers—those who have come forward with shocking claims of misconduct, manipulation, and abuse. These aren’t just sensational headlines—they are stories that expose a side of Sean Combs the public never saw. The Diddy Diaries explores how these accusations began to surface, what drove his accusers to finally speak out, and how the legal system has responded.

But this isn’t just about Diddy’s public image taking a hit—The Diddy Diaries zeroes in on the high-stakes battle that Diddy now faces to stay out of jail. Each episode unpacks the legal drama as it unfolds in real-time: the charges, the court proceedings, and the fight for his freedom. Once a man who had it all—fame, fortune, and influence—Diddy now stands at the center of a legal storm that threatens to strip him of everything, including his freedom.


We trace the events that led up to this moment, looking at how Diddy’s larger-than-life persona masked a pattern of behavior that would ultimately bring him to this point. Listeners will get a front-row seat to the high-stakes legal battles, where the possibility of incarceration looms large. The tension is palpable as Diddy’s legal team works to combat the mounting evidence against him, while his accusers push for justice.

Through riveting storytelling, The Diddy Diaries paints a full picture of Diddy’s downfall, from the heights of his stardom to the depths of his legal fight for survival. This is not a story about maintaining power or influence—it’s a fight to avoid the ultimate consequence: prison. As the allegations mount and the legal drama intensifies, The Diddy Diaries delivers a compelling narrative of a man grappling with the very real possibility of losing everything he once held dear.

With every episode, listeners are drawn deeper into this unfolding saga of celebrity, power, and justice. The Diddy Diaries not only explores the high-profile case but also delves into the human cost behind the headlines. As Diddy’s world unravels, so too does the carefully constructed image he spent decades building. This podcast is a must-listen for anyone who wants to understand the full scope of Sean Combs’ downfall, where the stakes couldn’t be higher and the outcome remains uncertain.

Prepare for a rollercoaster ride through the most shocking and consequential scandal in recent entertainment history. Tune in to The Diddy Diaries: The Downfall of Sean Combs to witness the dramatic fall of a man who once ruled the music world but now fights for his freedom in the face of life-altering allegations.

structure.rationale: The show meanders from legislative history to superhero films to drink recipes without clear transitions or deliberate pacing, making it difficult to identify a coherent throughline beyond a loose bar-chat format.

relevance.rationale: The episode touches on real political issues like executive overreach and congressional accountability, but quickly drifts into extended tangents about Superman movies, multitasking studies, and cocktail recipes, diluting the political substance significantly.

clipability.rationale: The most quotable political moment — 'the laws exist right now and they're not reigning him in' — requires surrounding context to land, and most other segments blend into casual banter that doesn't stand alone well.

card.tagline: Trump, Congress, and cocktails: when political frustration meets superhero trivia
card.description: A casual roundtable dissects why post-Watergate laws are failing to constrain the executive branch, then pivots to Superman, Supergirl, and a themed drink recipe.
card.reason: Because this episode debates whether existing laws can actually stop a president

clip.text: to bluff, to read incomplete information, to make decisions when you can't see all the cards. And what are they doing with that skill now? They're making money for quant hedge funds. The lab is now valued at more than five hundred million dollars. I love this one because it tells you something true about where AI talent actually goes. Everybody thinks the smart money is chasing the next chatbot, the next consumer app. But poker and markets are cousins. Both are games of incomplete information, probability, and opponents who are also trying to outwit you. The same math that figures out when to bluff in Texas Hold'em figures out

transcript.scoringWindow: at it. Let me set the table. Anthropic put out a new model called Claude Sonnet 5. The pitch is simple and it's important: this is the cheaper way to run agents. Not the flagship, not the show-off Opus tier — the workhorse. They're positioning it as a budget alternative to their own Opus, to GPT-5.5, and to Gemini Pro. Stronger agentic chops, lower pricing, better safety guardrails. That's the marketing line. Now here's where it gets interesting, and this is why I love when a careful person actually reads the fine print instead of just retweeting the press release. Simon Willison went and did the homework, and he found that the cheaper-model story has a wrinkle. Anthropic shipped Sonnet 5 with a new tokenizer. And the new tokenizer, by Willison's math, makes the model roughly one-point-four times more expensive for English, about one-point-three-three times more expensive for Spanish, and roughly the same price for Simplified Mandarin. Let me explain why that matters, because it's not a nerd footnote, it's a billing footnote. When you buy time from one of these models, you don't pay per word, you pay per token. A token is a chunk of text — sometimes a whole word, sometimes a piece of one. The tokenizer is the thing that decides how your sentence gets chopped into those chunks. So if the published price-per-token goes down but the tokenizer chops your English into more pieces, your actual bill can go up even though the sticker price went down. That's the trick of the tape here. A model can be advertised as cheaper and still cost you more per real-world paragraph, depending on what language you write in and how the tokenizer behaves. So for the founder building on this stuff, the lesson is don't read the headline price, run your own workload through it and watch the meter. Especially if your product is English-heavy, which most of you are. The advertised discount and the invoice you actually get at the end of the month are two different animals. I'm not saying Sonnet 5 is a bad deal — it may still be a great deal for agent workloads where you're firing thousands of small calls. I'm saying measure it yourself before you migrate your whole stack. And yes, Willison also generated his customary pelican on a bicycle to vibe-check the thing, because that's the man's tradition, and I respect a tradition. That tokenizer wrinkle is a perfect on-ramp to the real theme today, because it's all about the gap between what AI costs on paper and what it costs in practice — and nowhere is that gap bigger or more consequential than in the chips that run all of this. So let's talk about Etched. This is the story I think most builders should sit up for. Etched is one of these companies going after Nvidia, and the news is they've hit a five billion dollar valuation and — this is the number that matters — they say they've already booked one billion dollars under contract for the inference systems powered by their chip. One billion dollars of contracted revenue. Now let me give you the why, and I'll keep the chip-nerd stuff short because I promised you I would. Etched's whole bet is different from Nvidia's. Nvidia builds general-purpose chips — they can train models, they can run models, they can do a lot of different math. That flexibility is exactly why Nvidia owns the world right now. Etched went the other direction. They built a chip that does basically one thing: run transformer models — the kind that power Claude, GPT, Gemini, all of them — for inference. Inference is the part where the model is actually answering your customer, not the part where it's being trained. They baked the transformer architecture into the silicon itself. You give up flexibility, you can't pivot if the architecture changes, but in exchange you get speed and cost efficiency on the one job that ninety-nine percent of production AI workloads actually are. Here's why a billion in bookings is the headline and not the five billion valuation. We've spent the last couple weeks on this show talking about the AI economy from the cost side — the memory shortage, the RAMmageddon, South Korea throwing a trillion dollars at memory fabs, which we covered yesterday. The squeeze on everybody has been the cost of running these models. Inference cost is the single biggest variable in whether an AI company actually makes money. If your model is great but it costs you four dollars to serve an answer you charge three dollars for, you don't have a business, you have a charity with a chatbot. So when a specialized inference-chip company shows up with a billion dollars of real contracts — not hopes, not a pitch deck, contracts — that's a market telling you it will pay real money to get inference cheaper. That's demand validation for the entire idea that the cost curve has to come down, and somebody other than Nvidia might be the one to bend it. For the founder, the practical read is this: the inference layer is getting competitive, and that's good for you. You don't buy Etched chips yourself, you're not running a data center. But the people you rent compute from increasingly will have alternatives to Nvidia, and competition at that layer eventually shows up as lower prices in your bill — or at least as a brake on prices going up. Keep an eye on which clouds start offering inference on non-Nvidia silicon, because that's where your margins quietly improve. Now, let's shift from the chip running the model to the people trying to build a business on top of the model, because there's a great little story here about a poker AI. Three researchers who left DeepMind started a lab in Prague called EquiLibre Technologies. These are the folks who built poker-playing AI — the kind of system that learned to bluff, to read incomplete information, to make decisions when you can't see all the cards. And what are they doing with that skill now? They're making money for quant hedge funds. The lab is now valued at more than five hundred million dollars. I love this one because it tells you something true about where AI talent actually goes. Everybody thinks the smart money is chasing the next chatbot, the next consumer app. But poker and markets are cousins. Both are games of incomplete information, probability, and opponents who are also trying to outwit you. The same math that figures out when to bluff in Texas Hold'em figures out how to position a trade when you can't see what the other side is holding. So three people who could've gone and built another assistant instead took their decision-making-under-uncertainty expertise straight to the place that pays the most for exactly that skill: finance. For founders, here's the takeaway that's bigger than poker. The most valuable AI applications are often the ones that don't look like AI applications. Nobody's calling EquiLibre an AI company in the headlines the way they call OpenAI one. But the edge they built is the same edge — superhuman reasoning under uncertainty — pointed at a niche where that edge converts directly into dollars. If you're hunting for where to build, don't only look at the obvious chat-and-image stuff where you're competing with the giants. Look for the corners where reasoning under uncertainty has a clear price tag attached. Trading is one. There are others nobody's named yet. Which brings us to Anthropic again, because they're also trying to find a corner — and theirs is science. Anthropic launched something called Claude Science, and the framing in the reporting from Rebecca Bellan at TechCrunch is the smart part: Anthropic is betting on workflow, not a new model, to win over scientists. Let me unpack that. Claude Science is described as a workbench — one environment where a scientist can do computational research without bouncing between a dozen databases, pipelines, and tools. The pitch isn't "our model is smarter at chemistry." The pitch is "we'll save you from the death-by-a-thousand-tabs reality of actually doing computational science." Anybody who's worked next to a researcher knows the truth — half the job isn't the thinking, it's the plumbing. Getting the data out of one system, into another format, through a pipeline, into a tool, back out, into a chart. It's miserable and it eats days. So Anthropic's move here is notable precisely because it's not about the model. They didn't say "here's a science-tuned Claude that scores higher on some chemistry benchmark." They said "here's an environment." And that's a tell about where these labs think the value is heading. The raw model is increasingly a commodity — everybody's got one within six months of everybody else's. The thing you can actually charge a premium for, and the thing that's hard to switch away from, is the workflow you build around it. Once a lab full of scientists has all their pipelines living in Claude Science, they're not casually swapping that out next quarter because GPT got a point better on a benchmark. That's the same instinct, by the way, that we talked about a few episodes back when Anthropic was building Claude into Slack to capture institutional knowledge. It's a pattern with this company: don't just sell intelligence, sell the place where the work lives. For founders, if you're building an AI product and your only moat is "we use a good model," you have no moat, because so does the next guy. The moat is the workflow, the data that accumulates inside your product, the switching cost you create by becoming the place where the work happens. Anthropic clearly believes that, and they're putting product effort behind it. Now let me shift gears to something a lot less optimistic, because we should talk about the security story, and it's a doozy. Dan Goodin over at Ars Technica wrote up a new attack on AI browsers, and the one-line version is almost funny until you realize it isn't: telling an LLM that two plus two equals five is enough to make it follow forbidden instructions. The headline on the piece is blunt — it calls this one more reason why AI browsers are a bad idea. Here's the mechanism, in plain terms. An AI browser is a browser with an AI agent baked in that can read pages, click things, fill out forms, take actions on your behalf. The whole appeal is it does stuff for you. The new attack lulls the model into what the researchers describe as a kind of dream world where the guardrails stop applying. You feed it a premise that's false — two plus two is five — and get it to go along, to operate in this little fictional frame, and once it's accepted that frame, it'll follow instructions it would normally refuse. You've basically talked it out of reality, and a model that's out of reality is a model that's out of its safety training. Why does this matter for builders specifically? Because so many of you are racing to ship agentic features — agents that browse, that act, that touch your users' accounts and data. And the uncomfortable truth this attack underlines is that the guardrails on these things are not walls, they're suggestions written in chalk. If a false premise wrapped in the right story can walk a model past its own rules, then any agent you give real permissions to — read my email, make this purchase, move this money — is a soft target. The Ars piece is part of a steady drumbeat now. Just last week there was the report about coding agents being tricked into installing malware from what looked like clean GitHub repositories. Same family of problem: the agent's own helpfulness is the exploit. So my advice, and it's not glamorous: scope your agents

structure.rationale: Each story gets a clear setup, explanation, and founder takeaway, creating a consistent and navigable throughline, though the transitions between segments are conversational rather than formally signposted.

relevance.rationale: The episode covers AI industry developments (chip valuations, model pricing, security research) but has no connection to current news or political events — it's tech business commentary, not News/Politics content.

clipability.rationale: Several self-contained, quotable moments work as standalone excerpts — particularly the tokenizer billing explainer and the 'if your only moat is a good model, you have no moat' line — though most require at least minimal context.

card.tagline: The AI bill you get isn't the one you were quoted — here's why
card.description: Breaks down how Anthropic's cheaper model may actually cost more, why a poker AI is now worth $500M, and why AI agent guardrails are written in chalk.
card.reason: Because this episode unpacks the real economics behind AI tools founders are building on right now

scored.description:
"The Neutral Corner" boxing podcast covers all the latest news in the fight game. Every Monday, host Michael Montero reviews/previews all the fights from around the world and takes listener calls. He is never afraid to ruffle feathers when he gives hot takes or goes on heated rants.

Montero has contributed to numerous boxing publications in multiple countries, performed ringside commentary around the USA and is an International Boxing Hall of Fame voter.

latestEpisode.title: Episode 389: Jaron "Boots" Ennis stops Xander Zayas; Oleksandr Usyk vacates Titles

structure.rationale: There is a discernible throughline moving from intra-Democratic ideological conflict to third-party prospects to DSA victories to defund-the-police, but transitions are conversational and loose, with a mid-segment Anita Baker digression that disrupts the political flow.

relevance.rationale: The episode engages substantively with live political fault lines — DSA electoral wins, Democratic Party ideological fractures, defund-the-police debates, and third-party viability — but the published date of 2026-06-30 makes some references (Bernie 2020, early DSA wins) feel temporally ambiguous rather than sharply current.

clipability.rationale: Several passages — West's 'neoliberal plantation' line, 'if all we have is the Democratic Party you're gonna lead folk into the crackhouse,' and the Bernie's babies framing — are punchy and self-contained enough to work as standalone previews without heavy setup.

card.tagline: Cornel West calls the Democratic Party a neoliberal plantation — live on air
card.description: Tavis and Dr. West debate whether the Democratic Party can survive its left-right civil war, third-party viability, and what DSA wins mean for Black political power.
card.reason: Because this episode cuts to the heart of the Democratic Party's ideological crisis

Live from West Virginia’s Capital City, with stories from every corner of the Mountain State. From Noon to 3 p.m., MetroNews Midday connects West Virginians with the news that matters most — hosted by 13 News and Tonight Live anchor Amanda Barren and Dave Allen of MetroNews. Amanda and Dave deliver up-to-the-minute headlines and breaking news while amplifying voices from around the state through insightful interviews and real-time conversation.

📺 Watch Hour 3 live on any device with MetroNews TV
📱 Join the conversation by texting 304-TALK-304 or call 800-765-TALK

 With featured segments like:
  • Mayor Monday – spotlighting towns and cities across the state
  • Wonderful West Virginian – honoring unsung heroes for their generosity and community spirit
MetroNews Midday is your live, local, and lively midday connection to West Virginia.

relevance.rationale: The episode centers on the same-day U.S. Supreme Court ruling in Becky Pepper Jackson v. Chi ha cancro o ha una crash di questo segno tende a vivere le emozioni in modo profondo e cerca sempre amore e dolcezza nelle relazioni. Secondo le stelle, la compatibilità migliore in amore per il cancro si trova con lo scorpione, i pesci e il toro. Con lo scorpione nasce una connessione intensa e profonda. Vi sentite protetti e riuscite a tirar fuori il meglio l'uno dall'altro. nonostante qualche piccolo momento di gerosia. Con i pesci c'è una sintonia emotiva unica, fatta di coccole e sostegno reciproco, che vi permette di vivere una storia dolce e sognante.

Beyond the Horizon is a project that aims to dig a bit deeper than just the surface level that we are so used to with the legacy media while at the same time attempting to side step the gaslighting and rhetoric in search of the truth. From the day to day news that dominates the headlines to more complex geopolitical issues that effect all of our lives, we will be exploring them all.

It's time to stop settling for what is force fed to us and it's time to look beyond the horizon.

structure.rationale: The episode follows a loose quote-then-react format but lacks clear segments, frequently digresses, and has no discernible intro or close in the excerpt.

relevance.rationale: Directly engages a current news hook — Lutnick's public statements and the July 2026 DOJ/FBI memo — making it timely, though the underlying Epstein story is years old.

clipability.rationale: Several punchy standalone moments exist — particularly the 'greatest blackmailer' framing and the Bill Gates 'why not sue somebody' riff — though most require at least minimal setup.

structure.rationale: The episode moves between distinct interview segments with identifiable guests and clear topic transitions, but pacing is uneven and commercial breaks interrupt the flow without strong signposting between segments.

relevance.rationale: The episode covers two timely, specific local and regional news events — a deadly marina fire in Cabo San Lucas and earthquake relief efforts in Venezuela — giving it clear current-events grounding, though the scope is hyperlocal rather than broadly political.

clipability.rationale: The fire chief's account of the marina blaze and the Venezuela corruption commentary both contain self-contained, quotable moments, though most require at least minimal setup to land fully.

card.tagline: A yacht fire, a sunken fireboat, and a death at Cabo's marina
card.description: Cabo San Lucas's fire chief details a four-hour marina blaze that killed one person and nearly sank Mexico's first purpose-built marine firefighting vessel, while guests discuss Venezuela's earthquake response and government obstruction of aid.
card.reason: Because this episode breaks down two urgent disasters unfolding right now

relevance.rationale: The episode directly addresses the active US-Iran-Israel conflict, nuclear negotiations, Strait of Hormuz strategy, and Trump's diplomatic approach — all timely geopolitical developments, though the conversation meanders and lacks precise news hooks.

card.tagline: An Egyptian-born doctor explains what Iran actually wants from these negotiations
card.description: Dr. Mark Christian breaks down Iran's real agenda — legitimacy, sanctions relief, and regional power — and why Trump may be uniquely positioned to deliver it.
card.reason: Because this episode unpacks the Iran nuclear talks from a rare insider cultural perspective

scored.feedTitle: Good News York by Growth Mode Content
scored.description:
Your go-to podcast for inspiring stories, uplifting news, and positive vibes from across New York State!

Whether it's groundbreaking innovations, community heroes, or businesses making a difference, we bring you the stories that remind us all of the good happening around us.

Join us as we highlight the changemakers, dreamers, and everyday folks bringing positivity to the Empire State.

From local businesses thriving in Syracuse to heartwarming tales from Buffalo, Albany, and beyond—this is the news you’ll actually want to hear. Because good news deserves to be heard.

🔊 Listen in, get inspired, and spread the good! Good News York is a project of Growth Mode Content in Syracuse, NY.

Visit: GoodNewsYork.com for full video episodes, clips, links and more!

Want to create podcasts and content for your brand? That's what Growth Mode Content is all about! Visit: GrowthModeContent.com to learn more!

Growth Mode Content is a division of Growth Mode Technologies LLC.

transcript.scoringWindow: The political and democratic infrastructure of Peru experienced a historic yet deeply polarizing shift as the official electoral agency completed the full count of the high stakes presidential runoff. Conservative leader Keiko Fujimori has emerged victorious on her fourth consecutive bid for the highest office, securing the slimmest of margins against her left wing rival, Roberto Sanchez. The dramatic resolution caps more than three weeks of intense ballot reviews and bitter allegations of irregularities, returning the highly controversial Fujimori dynasty back to the executive palace in Lima. The final tally released by the National Office of Electoral Processes confirmed an incredibly tight finish that exposes a nation fractured entirely down the middle. Out of more than eighteen million ballots cast, Fujimori captured fifty point one four percent of the vote, edging out Sanchez's forty nine point eight seven percent by a razor thin margin of fewer than fifty thousand votes. The left wing candidate initially took an early lead as rural domestic precincts were tallied, but the momentum completely inverted as heavily conservative overseas ballots, particularly from Peruvian expatriates living in the United States, were progressively processed. Addressing an anxious crowd of reporters outside her residence following the final tally, the fifty one year old president elect struck an uncharacteristically measured tone regarding the immense task ahead. Fujimori acknowledged that the country is practically divided and emphasized that she bears a profound responsibility to listen to citizens from both sides of the political spectrum, proclaiming that her doors to dialogue remain wide open to Sanchez and competing parliamentary factions. She identified her administrations immediate twin priorities as restoring public safety and rapidly deploying preventative economic measures against the looming al Nino weather phenomenon. Historic victory makes Fujimori the first elected female president in Peruvian history, cementing a broader right wing resurgence sweeping across major South American economies. Campaigning heavily under a hardline platform focused on tackling the country's soaring extortion and organized crime crises, Fujimori frequently invoked the iron fisted security legacy of her late father, former autocratic president Alberto Fujimori. However, managing the deeply fractured state will present an immediate challenge, as the country has cycled through eight different presidents over the last decade alone, and Sanchez's camp has already vowed to form a coalition of resistance to aggressively challenge the final certification

clipability.rationale: The vote-margin detail and 'first female president in Peruvian history' moment are genuinely self-contained hooks, but most passages assume the listener is already tracking the story and require the surrounding setup to land fully.

card.reason: Because a historic, disputed South American election just reached its dramatic conclusion

scored.description:
The Vault: The Epstein Files Unsealed is a deep-dive investigative podcast that pulls back the curtain on one of the most protected criminal networks in modern history. This series is built from the ground up on the actual paper trail—unsealed court records, depositions, exhibits, emails, and filings that were never meant to be read by the public. No pundit panels. No spin. Just the documents themselves, examined line by line, name by name, connection by connection—paired with precise, document-driven analysis that explains what the record truly shows.

Each episode opens the vault on newly unsealed or long-buried Epstein files and walks listeners through what they actually reveal about power, money, influence, and the systems that failed survivors at every turn. Alongside the filings themselves, informed commentary breaks down the legal strategy, the institutional behavior, the contradictions, and the implications hiding between the lines. From judges’ orders and sealed exhibits to sworn testimony and back-channel communications, the show connects the dots the media often won’t—or can’t. Patterns emerge. Timelines collapse. Excuses fall apart.

The Vault is a working archive in audio form, a living record of the Epstein case as told by the courts themselves—supplemented by rigorous analysis that provides context, challenges official narratives, and exposes where the record has been distorted, sanitized, or deliberately ignored. Every claim is grounded in filings. Every episode is anchored to the record. Listeners aren’t told what to think—they are shown what exists, what was said under oath, and what the commentary reveals about how those facts were buried, softened, or misrepresented.

If you want to understand how Jeffrey Epstein was protected, who circled him, how institutions closed ranks, and why accountability keeps slipping through the cracks, The Vault: The Epstein Files Unsealed is where the record finally speaks for itself—and where the commentary ensures the documents do what no press release ever will.

transcript.scoringWindow: The time is now six oh one p m. In New York. I'm Noah Levi and this is the latest Israel Today, ongoing war report. In Israeli domestic politics, Prime Minister Benjamin Netania, who said Tuesday night that voluntary migration of Gaza residents remains on the table and he did not rule out renewed Jewish settlement in the Strip. In a special interview with The Patriots on Channel fourteen, he was asked whether migration and the possibility of re establishing Jewish communities in Gaza were still on the agenda. Netania, who framed the question as a choice between action and talk, saying the question is whether you prefer to do or to talk. He clarified that voluntary migration is still on the table, and while he previously described rebuilding settlements as unrealistic, he avoided giving a direct yes or no on re establishing communities in Gaza, a stance that drew scrutiny from his right wing base in antisemitism and anti Jewish hate. The San Francisco trans March downplayed the harassment of LGBTQ Jewish congressional candidate Scott Wiener at their event last weekend. Organizers told the Jewish Telegraphic Agency that many working class LGBTQ people believe Wiena does not represent their interests. A viral video captured Wiena being taunted with references to Israel and Gaza as he left a Dolores Park gathering. After the confrontation, the organizers said Wiener is criticized by his constituents everywhere he goes, and noted that he has made choices that have led many in the community to feel unrepresented. They did not sign their names to the statement. In Israeli Economy and Business, a National Digital Network report submitted to the Knesset shows that sixty five percent of public services provided by state agencies are digitized, with one hundred and sixteen that should have been digitized by law still pending. The report maps forty five sixty two public services across government ministries, support units, corporations and authorities. More than half of the services are available in digital format, and common digital tasks include renewing a driver's license, applying for an identity card, obtaining a disability certificate, and registering for day care. Additionally, one hundred and thirty eight of the pending services are accessible via email. Seventy local authorities, representing twenty seven percent of all local authorities, ask the network for help digitizing their services, with the majority coming from low socioconomic clusters and about thirty percent from the non Jewish sector. In uplifting news, Jonathan Spector's Birthright Now at mcc Theatre in Manhattan, uses the Free Israel Trip as the starting point for decades of wrestling with Israel, Judaism, and friendship. Specter, who has staffed thirteen Birthright trips and sent more than five hundred young Jews to Israel, watches six American Jews across three moments in time two thousand and six after Birthright, twenty sixteen after d a wedding rehearsal dinner, and twenty twenty four after a Shiva confront how the Israel conversation has evolved over eighteen years, linking the trip to real life journeys and the changing arc of identity. Separately, Israeli's a choosing World Cup favorites as they follow the twenty twenty six tournament without a national team of their own. In screenings around the country, many route for other nations, often based on roots and players, with a Tel Aviv organized event featuring fans in Rinaldo's Portugal jersey, and a local poll showing sixty one percent plan to follow the tournament at some level and thirty four percent of men planning to watch every game, compared with eight percent of women. Thank you for tuning in to this Israel Today Ongoing War Report update. I'm Noah Levi. Stay safe and informed. Keep in mind that this AAR generated report may contain occasional inaccuracies, so consult multiple sources for a comprehensive view. Find the code and more details in the podcast description.

scored.description:
Melissa Unfiltered - a podcast where nothing is off limits. As a Mom with three adopted kids, I dive into foster care system failures, realities of Mom life, homeschooling, sexual abuse cover ups, domestic violence and any issues that get me fired up each week.

My perspective is unfiltered, fearless, and usually different from the mainstream narrative. 

Join me as I question and expose our cultures “accepted” truths, shine a light on the uncomfortable, and explore topics through a lens that’s without boundaries and uniquely mine.

structure.rationale: The episode follows a loose narrative arc from personal backstory to the scam mechanics to bank accountability, but pacing is uneven, tangents are frequent, and there is no clear segmenting or signposting.

relevance.rationale: The episode covers a real and growing consumer fraud issue but frames it entirely as a personal family story with no connection to current legislation, regulatory action, or political developments.

clipability.rationale: The core scam explanation — searching a bank's phone number and losing $50,000 — is a genuinely self-contained, alarming moment, but it requires several minutes of family backstory before it lands and trails off without a punchy close.

card.tagline: Your grandparents could lose $50K just by Googling their bank
card.description: Melissa's grandparents lost their life savings to scammers after a routine internet search — and she argues the system is letting it happen.
card.reason: Because this episode exposes a scam tactic most people have never heard of

Each week the All Caught Up team examine a newsworthy topic, talk to those in the know and consider what it means for young Australians and beyond.

Sound Editor and Engineer: Michelle Zeng  @michelle.zeng.creative

Executive producer: Alexandra Spring  @newsworthy_aus

structure.rationale: The episode follows a clear throughline — incident, cause, technology, fear, economy, debate — with identifiable segments and a conversational but purposeful progression, though pacing occasionally drifts into casual tangents.

relevance.rationale: The episode ties directly to a specific June 2026 shark attack and ongoing public safety debate in Australia, with timely data and recent political/environmental hooks, though it skews more toward feature journalism than hard news or political analysis.

clipability.rationale: The expert audio from Ian Southers on ocean currents and the drone-flashing-surfers anecdote are genuinely self-contained moments, but most exchanges require the surrounding conversation to land fully.

card.tagline: Shark attacks are spiking in Sydney — is culling the only answer?
card.description: After a woman lost her arm to a great white in June 2026, hosts investigate climate change, drone tech, and the political firestorm over shark culling.
card.reason: Because this episode unpacks a live public safety and environmental policy debate

scored.description: Plugged In Australia is your essential podcast for the latest electric vehicle news tailored to Aussie drivers. We break down fresh updates on sales trends, policy changes like road-user charges and tax exemptions, and infrastructure developments—from charging networks in Sydney to regional rollouts. Get quick insights on new models hitting the market, like affordable BYD imports and Tesla’s latest, plus analysis on how global shifts affect Oz. Whether you’re tracking EV adoption rates or debunking myths, tune in weekly for concise, no-fluff coverage to keep you informed on the road to a greener future. Subscribe now and plug into the conversation

transcript.scoringWindow: Gday, welcome to Plugged in Australia Quick Charge, episode 68 for Wednesday, the 1st of July 2026. And today weve got a cheaper Cooper Tavascan landing in Australia, the countrys first locally made electric Bendy bus hitting the road in Perth, BMW locking in a full electric IX5 for Australia, GWMs new Haval H10 plug-in hybrid flagship, a big Volvo EX90 price cut, Hyundai calling a turning point for EV sales, Chinas oldest car brand heading our way, and BYD already preparing a major rework of the ADO2. Lets get into it. Alright, first up, Cooper has made the Tavascun a much more interesting proposition in Australia. Theres now an A new entry-level version called the Tavascan V. Itll be priced from $55.490 before on-road costs. It makes it about five and a half grand cheaper than the previous entry point to the range, and importantly, it also puts the Tavascan below the Tesla Model Y on starting price. But as always, theres a bit of a trade-off. The new V uses a 58kWh LFP battery, a single rear motor making 140kW, and has a WLTP driving range of 414 kilometers. DC charging is capped at 105kW with a claimed 10-80% charge time of 26 minutes. So thats not as powerful and doesnt go as far as the larger battery versions, but the equipment list is actually pretty strong. You still get heated and power adjustable front seats, a 12-speaker Sannheiser sound system, surround view camera, ambient lighting, and a fairly premium cabin. Kruppers also made some important updates across the range. The tiny 5.3 inch driver display has now been replaced by a larger 10.25 inch display, and Cooper has swapped out the controversial haptic steering wheel controls for physical buttons. That alone will make the car feel a lot less annoying day to day. The Tavaskan Endurance remains in the range with the bigger 77 kWh battery, 210 kW of power, 545 Nm, and up to 520km of WLTP range. The VZ keeps the dual motor all-wheel drive setup with 250 kW and 508 Nm. Fact of the matter is the new V makes the Tavascan a little bit easier to justify, but it doesnt fix every issue. The four-star NCAP rating will still put some buyers off, and Cooper still needs to keep improving the digital and ownership side of the car, but at this new price its absolutely more competitive. And next a story that isnt about a passenger car, but its still a big deal for electrification in Australia. Perth now has Australias first locally made electric articulated bus, or Bendy bus as we like to call them, hitting the road. Its been built at a Volgrens Malagar facility in WA using Volvos BZL electric articulated platform. It has it is 18 meters long, seats 57 passengers, and can carry more than 100 people when standing passengers are included. Underneath it has a 497 kWh LFP battery and dual electric motors, producing up to 400 kW. Charging takes around 4 hours, which makes sense for a depot-based public transport route where buses can return to base and charge on schedule. Testing will happen first in Perths northern suburbs, with full service expected around August. This is exactly the sort of EV story that deserves a little bit more attention. Its not as flashy as a new performance SUV, but public transport is where electrification can make a massive real-world difference. You get low running costs, less maintenance, quieter streets, and in this case, local manufacturing as well. Now to BMW, and this big one for the luxury SUV market, thats for sure. The next generation BMW X5 has been locked in for Australia. And for the first time, the X5 family will now include a full battery electric version called the iX5. Its original, isnt it? The electric version is expected here in the first quarter of 2027, with Australian production timing beginning earlier next year. The headline figure is range. BMW is talking up to 845 kilometres of WLTP range from a massive battery pack. Wow, 141 kilowatt hours. Thats just humongous. Puts the IX5 firmly into long-distance luxury EV territory. The IX5 60X drive is expected to produce around 425 kilowatts, do naught to 100 in about 4.6 seconds, and will run on an 800 volt electric architecture with DC charging capability up to 460 kilowatts. The obvious catch here is that Australia does not have many chargers that can actually deliver that sort of speed today, but as a future-proof system, its pretty impressive. Even if youre using lower-powered ultra-rapid chargers locally, the benefit should be a strong charging curve and less weighting on road trips. So should hold those 250 kilowatt rates for a little bit longer than some of the other ones. The other thing to consider is weight. The iX5 is expected to be just under three tons. So this is not some lightweight efficiency hero. Its a big luxury SUV with a massive battery designed to remove range anxiety for buyers who want an X5 but electric. The new X5 range wont be EV only either. Petrol, diesel, and plug-in hybrid versions are also coming. The plug-in hybrid is expected to offer somewhere around 86 to 102 kilometers of electric driving range. That will depend on the version, of course, which is exactly the sort of FEV setup that can make sense for family buyers doing mostly local trips but still wanting easy long distance capability. Although Im not sure how much easier its going to get than uh 400 odd kilowatt charging. But anyway, GWMs Havel brand has also revealed more of its new H10 flagship plug-in hybrid SUV. This is a big, boxy, off-road-styled SUV aimed at the upper large end of the market. You think Toyota Prado, Nissan Patrol, Land Rover Defender, Denzer B8, and the Tank 500 sort of territory. It is not confirmed for Australia yet, so dont treat this as a local locked-in launch, but it is relevant because GWM Australia has been openly looking at where models like this could fit into the scope. The H10 is expected to be offered with five-seat and six-seat layouts. The five seat version is just over 5.1 metres long, while the six-seat version stretches to almost 5.3 metres. It uses GWMs HI4 plug-in system with a 42.8 kWh and a claimed electric range of up to 180km on our fantastic CLTC cycle. The range figure will almost certainly be lower under WLTP or even real-world conditions, but even allowing for that, it is a proper plug-in battery size, not a token hybrid battery. Inside, it gets the current Chinese flagship treatment, big floating touchscreen, digital instrument display, physical controls in the centre area, wireless charging, a built-in fridge, and even a roof-mounted rear passenger screen. For Australia, the interesting question is whether GWM brings this in under Haval or keeps that upper large off-road space for tank. Either way, the plug-in hybrid off-road SUV battle is starting to heat up pretty quickly. For model year 2027, the EX90 range will now start with a new Plus single motor rear-wheel drive variant, priced from $106.99 before on-road costs. Its about $18,000 cheaper than the previous entry point, which is the Plus twin motor all-wheel drive. The new base EX90 uses a 92kWh battery, has 245 kilowatts and 480 nm of torque, and offers up to 479km of WLTP range. Does 0 to 100 in 6.8 seconds compared with the previous entry all-wheel drive version? You lose power, you lose the front motor, and you lose some battery capacity and range. So again, its not a free discount. It is Volvo creating a lower cost version to bring more people into the EX90 family. But the equipment level is still strong. You get the big central touchscreen, Google built-in, wireless smartphone mirroring, a bow sound system, four zone climate control, heated front and second row seats, a heated steering wheel, panoramic roof, power tailgate, and all the usual Volvo safety focus. The EX90 is still not cheap, and the Kia EV9 will start below it, but this makes Volvos big electric 7 seater feel a little bit more realistic than it did before. It also undercuts the Cadillac Vistic, which matters because the luxury 3 row EV space is about to get a lot more competitive. Hyundai Australia says EV sales here have turned a corner, and honestly, looking at the recent numbers, you can understand why they may say this. As weve covered recently, EV market share in Australia hit 16.4% in April and 19.9% in May, helped by a huge month for the Tesla Model Y. Hyundai says its EV supply is increasing, with shipments moving towards around 1,000 EVs per month. Its own EV sales are up strongly year on year, and the Conor Electric has been doing a lot of the heavy lifting. The bigger picture here is that EV demand has not disappeared. Its just become more price sensitive and more supply sensitive. When the right cars arrive at the right prices, buyers are clearly still there. The FBT exemption is also still a major factor for Novated Lease buyers, and that is one reason that electric cars are punching above their weight in the private and salary packaging market. Hyundai also has more product coming. The Ionic 9 gives it a proper three-row electric SUV, the Alexio, is expected to give Hyundai a stronger mainstream electric offering, and the smaller Ionic 3 should give it a more affordable EV option when it arrives. Another Chinese brand is preparing to enter Australia, but this one is a very

structure.rationale: Each story is clearly delineated with a named vehicle or brand as a segment header, and the host follows a consistent intro-detail-assessment pattern throughout, though pacing is dense and unvaried.

relevance.rationale: This is an EV automotive news podcast with no connection to news/politics; it covers car pricing and product launches, not current events or political developments.

clipability.rationale: Several punchy, self-contained observations — the Tavascan undercutting Tesla Model Y on price, the BMW iX5's 845km range claim, and the EV market share figures — work as standalone moments without much setup.

card.tagline: Australia's EV market heats up with cheaper luxury SUVs and record sales
card.description: Seven EV stories in one hit: Tavascan undercuts Tesla, BMW locks in an electric X5, Volvo slashes EX90 pricing, and Australia's EV share nears 20%.
card.reason: Because this episode tracks every major EV launch and price move in Australia right now

structure.rationale: The excerpt is a loosely moderated conversation that drifts across trafficking, transgender custody cases, UK grooming gangs, and anti-communism without clear segmentation or deliberate pacing.

card.tagline: A genocide survivor's daughter says she sees Cambodia's warning signs in America today
card.description: Seak Smith draws parallels between Khmer Rouge tactics and what she calls ideological capture of U.S. child-protection systems, trafficking courts, and family law.
card.reason: Because this episode connects historical genocide to current child-protection debates

scored.description:
Welcome to the official podcast series from USA Cabinet Store – your premier destination for exceptional kitchen and bathroom remodeling since 2011. Based at usacabinetstore.com, we're passionate about bringing your dream home to life through functional, spectacularly designed spaces that enhance your well-being and family lifestyle.

Our Philosophy in Action "We believe that everyone deserves a beautiful home with functional, spectacularly designed kitchens and bathrooms. Our aim is to convert that dream into reality". This core philosophy drives every episode as we share expert insights, client success stories, and industry trends that matter to homeowners.

What to Expect from Our Podcast Join our team of kitchen and bathroom design professionals and installation experts as we explore:

  • Real Client Transformations: Hear directly from satisfied customers like Mrs. Naffa, who praised our "attention to detail and overall project overview"
  • Design Inspiration: From custom cabinet installations to premium countertop selections and space optimization solutions
  • Industry Expertise: Insights from our 16 showrooms across Virginia, Maryland, Texas, North Carolina, and Florida
  • Remodeling Tips: Professional guidance on everything from 3D rendering to complete home remodeling projects

Why Trust USA Cabinet Store With our recent achievement of a 4.8-star Trust Index rating, we've proven our commitment to excellence in both design and construction. Our comprehensive service portfolio includes complete kitchen and bathroom remodeling projects, from initial design consultation through final installation.

Locations & Community Focus Broadcasting from our showrooms in Fairfax and Chantilly, Virginia, we serve communities throughout Northern Virginia including Aldie, Alexandria, Ashburn, Burke, Centreville, Herndon, McLean, Reston, and beyond. Our expansion across multiple states reflects our national reach while maintaining local values and personalized service.

Connect with Us Visit usacabinetstore.com to explore our full range of services, browse our project galleries, and schedule your free design consultation. Follow our podcast journey as we continue to make homes better and improve lives through exceptional kitchen and bathroom transformations.

structure.rationale: The episode follows a tight, deliberate Q&A format moving logically from per-square-foot pricing through component costs, bathroom types, regional variation, financing, ROI, labor trends, materials, and tech — each segment clearly signposted.

relevance.rationale: This is a home improvement cost breakdown episode with no connection to news or political events; the tariff mention is brief and framed as a budgeting tip, not political analysis.

clipability.rationale: Several self-contained stat-driven moments work as standalone clips — the ROI breakdown by bathroom type and the 'best vs. worst ROI splurges' segment especially — though most require at least minimal framing around the cost figures.

relevance.rationale: The episode touches on genuinely timely tech-policy issues like AI export controls and data center regulation, but the News/Politics category is diluted by extended whiskey tasting, banter, and DVD-collecting tangents that crowd out substantive political analysis.

scored.description:
The Mike O'Meara Show has been delivering daily doses of laughter and insight since 2009. Hosted by the dynamic duo Mike O'Meara and Robb Spewak, this talk show dives deep into everything from personal stories to the latest in news and pop culture, all with a uniquely irreverent twist.
Transitioning from traditional radio to podcasting, The Mike O'Meara Show has cultivated a devoted fanbase that spans the country. Their success is undeniable.
What truly sets The Mike O'Meara Show apart is the strong connection with its listeners. The show thrives on the support and loyalty of a passionate community that tunes in daily for laughs, banter, and a sense of belonging.
For fans of talk radio, The Don and Mike Show, Opie and Anthony, Ron and Fez, Howard Stern, and more, this is the podcast that keeps the spirit of great radio alive. Join The Mike O'Meara Show family today and discover why it’s a beloved staple in the podcasting world.

structure.rationale: The episode is an unbroken, stream-of-consciousness recitation of religious epithets with no discernible segments, transitions, or narrative arc beyond alphabetical listing.

relevance.rationale: Despite a title listing current events like NBA free agency, SCOTUS, and Wimbledon, the episode content is entirely a religious recitation of biblical names and titles for Jesus Christ with no connection to any news or political topics.

clipability.rationale: Every moment is structurally identical — a continuous list of names — making it impossible to extract a self-contained 30-45 second clip that would function as a meaningful standalone preview.

card.tagline: An extended devotional recitation of hundreds of biblical names for Jesus Christ
card.description: Host Jarvis Kingston delivers an uninterrupted spoken-word litany of scriptural titles and epithets for Jesus, framed as praise and worship.
card.reason: Because this episode is a faith-based devotional, not a news or politics show

scored.feedTitle: The Last Days Of Jeffrey Epstein
scored.description:
The Last Days of Jeffrey Epstein is a long-form investigative podcast that serves as both an archive and a real-time chronicle of the events surrounding the life, imprisonment, and death of Jeffrey Epstein. From the moment of his arrest in 2019 through the unanswered questions that continue to emerge years later, the podcast follows the timeline piece by piece — examining court filings, jail records, witness statements, surveillance failures, media reports, government investigations, and newly released documents as they become public. Rather than treating the story as a closed chapter, the series approaches it as an evolving historical event whose consequences continue to ripple through politics, finance, intelligence circles, law enforcement, and the media. Every episode is designed to preserve the record while also analyzing how the narrative around Epstein has shifted over time, what information has surfaced, what remains hidden, and why the case continues to generate worldwide scrutiny.

The podcast blends archival storytelling with ongoing investigative coverage, creating a living timeline of one of the most controversial deaths in modern American history. Using court documents, deposition transcripts, inspector general reports, leaked communications, news coverage, and firsthand accounts, The Last Days of Jeffrey Epstein reconstructs the chain of events that led to Epstein’s death inside the Metropolitan Correctional Center in New York while continuing to track the fallout that still unfolds in real time. The series does not simply revisit headlines; it documents how the story evolved, how institutions responded, how narratives changed, and how new revelations continue to reshape public understanding years later. Whether examining the failures inside MCC, the powerful figures connected to Epstein, or the unanswered questions surrounding the final days of his life, the podcast functions as both a historical archive and an ongoing examination of a case that refuses to disappear.

structure.rationale: The episode follows a loose Q&A transcript format that is functional but largely unguided — there is no clear host framing, segmentation, or signposting to help listeners navigate the dense documentary material.

relevance.rationale: The episode covers a specific, documented OIG investigation into Epstein's death at MCC, which remains a subject of ongoing public and political interest, though the granular procedural detail dilutes the broader news hook.

clipability.rationale: Nearly every exchange requires prior context about the documents being reviewed, the parties involved, and the investigative timeline, making standalone 30-45 second clips almost impossible to construct without heavy setup.

card.tagline: The OIG grills MCC's warden on who was watching Epstein — and why
card.description: A verbatim dive into the federal investigation transcript reveals logging errors, a suspicious guard overlap, and the difference between suicide watch and psych observation at MCC.
card.reason: Because this episode surfaces never-fully-explained procedural failures in Epstein's death

structure.rationale: The excerpt moves from casual banter to a traffic break to a sponsored guest interview with no clear editorial throughline or deliberate structural signposting.

relevance.rationale: The episode is a local Cincinnati morning radio show featuring a sponsored health clinic interview and tailgating small talk — no connection to current news or political events.

clipability.rationale: The 'health span vs. lifespan' distinction offers one quotable moment, but most exchanges require host relationship context or are mid-conversation sponsor reads that don't stand alone.

card.tagline: A Cincinnati doctor explains why your annual checkup is basically useless
card.description: Quotient Health co-founder Justin Grywee joins the show to pitch AI-driven biomarker testing as the alternative to the broken 15-minute yearly physical.
card.reason: Because this episode covers a local health trend gaining national attention

scored.description:
Since July 2017, Pete Quiñones has been the host of The Pete Quiñones Show (previously known as Free Man Beyond the Wall). His program has focused on the opposition to the globalist project that has undermined historical endeavors. His current interests include philosophy and historical revisionism.

Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/the-pete-quinones-show--6071361/support.

structure.rationale: The host acknowledges the episode is organized chronologically through a C-SPAN interview rather than by thematic logic, resulting in loose, digressive pacing with tangents that interrupt the main thread.

relevance.rationale: The episode engages substantively with Mossad operations, US-Israel relations, and Israeli political history through a book discussion, but the primary frame is literary analysis of a 30-year-old memoir rather than a response to a current breaking news hook.

clipability.rationale: There are several punchy standalone lines — the 'free trade' joke, the Jonathan Pollard anecdote, the underwing fuel tank scheme — but most require surrounding context about Ostrovsky or the book to land fully.

card.tagline: How Mossad turned American Jewish communities into a global logistics network
card.description: A deep dive into Victor Ostrovsky's second Mossad exposé reveals alleged Israeli trade schemes, Sayanim recruitment, and the political lineage connecting the Stern Gang to Netanyahu.
card.reason: Because this episode dissects covert Israeli intelligence operations and their American entanglements

scored.feedTitle: America’s Criminologist with Dr. Currie Myers
scored.description:
Dr. Currie Myers brings 35 years of hard‑earned expertise to every discussion—from his early days as a Kansas State Trooper and 
KBI Special Agent serving as sheriff of one of the largest counties in the Midwest.
 
As an applied criminologist, educator, ethicist and public‑policy expert, he has worked deep undercover on major drug operations, 
cracked public‑corruption rings and led high‑profile homicide investigations.
 
Tune in to America’s Criminologist with Dr. Currie Myers—where Sheriff (Ret.) Currie Myers, PhD, MBA, cuts through the noise on crime, policy and politics.
 
As a nationally renowned applied criminologist, ethicist and “go‑to” media expert, he brings hard‑won insight, fearless opinions and a dash 
of wit to every candid conversation. Ready for a real talk on the issues that shape justice in America? Dr. C’s got you covered.  https://drcurriemyers.substack.com/

structure.rationale: There is a discernible conversational throughline moving from retirement reflections to training gaps to constitutional policing to political interference, but transitions are loose and the dialogue meanders without clear segment breaks or signposting.

relevance.rationale: The episode touches on timely themes — constitutional policing, political pressure on police chiefs, and America's 250th anniversary — but centers more on career retrospective and general law enforcement philosophy than breaking news or a specific current political event.

clipability.rationale: Several genuinely standalone moments exist — particularly the barber-vs-officer training hours comparison and the 'guardrails on a bowling alley' Constitution analogy — that would work as short preview clips without requiring surrounding context.

card.tagline: A retired chief explains why cops forget how much power they actually hold
card.description: Former four-department police chief Scott Hughes breaks down officer training failures, constitutional limits on policing, and how mayors are quietly pressuring chiefs to enforce political agendas.
card.reason: Because this episode challenges how political pressure is reshaping American policing

scored.feedTitle: Watchdog on Wall Street with Chris Markowski

Welcome to The Watchdog on Wall Street Podcast. Author, investment banker, consumer advocate, analyst, and trader Chris Markowski exposes the lies and myths of the big brokerage firms, the mainstream press, and the government. Chris explains the news coming out of the complex worlds of finance, economics and politics and the impact it will have on everyday Americans.

For more information, visit radioamerica.com/watchdog-on-wall-street/

structure.rationale: The episode meanders through anecdotes about travel, marriage, Italy, and budgeting without clear segments, signposting, or a discernible arc beyond a general anti-retirement-book sentiment.

relevance.rationale: The episode is a loosely finance-adjacent lifestyle commentary with no specific current events, legislation, or market developments anchoring it to a timely news or politics hook.

clipability.rationale: A few punchy one-liners exist ('your life is not a fricking Excel spreadsheet') but most moments require the surrounding ramble to land, limiting standalone preview value.

card.tagline: Chris Markowski says retirement books are ruining how Americans think about money
card.description: Markowski argues that rigid retirement planning and budgeting culture rob people of joy, making the case for lifelong engagement over a magic finish-line date.
card.reason: Because this episode challenges conventional retirement wisdom with a contrarian take

structure.rationale: Conversational and digressive, with the host frequently losing the thread and self-correcting, though a loose chronological throughline holds the discussion together.

relevance.rationale: The episode covers historical Chinese political economy from 1949 onward — substantive but not tied to any current news hook or timely political development.

clipability.rationale: Most insights are buried mid-ramble and require surrounding context to land; the CIA/Golden Triangle passage is the closest thing to a standalone moment but still needs setup.

card.tagline: How the CCP rebuilt China from opium epidemic to industrial powerhouse
card.description: A deep-dive conversation tracing how Communist China eliminated the opium trade, launched Soviet-backed industrialization, and built a nationwide governing apparatus from scratch after 1949.
card.reason: Because this episode unpacks the historical roots of China's modern political system

scored.description:
Jeffrey Epstein: The Coverup Chronicles is a podcast dedicated to examining not just who Epstein was and what he did, but how so many people and institutions worked—then and now—to keep it all hidden. This series cuts past the headlines and digs into the documentation: court filings, deposition transcripts, plea deals, sealed exhibits, and the bureaucratic paper trail that still tells the real story. Our focus isn’t on speculation or recycled outrage. It’s on facts—and the deliberate efforts to keep those facts out of public view.

Each episode will feature in-depth analysis of newly surfaced records and underreported legal developments, alongside expert commentary that connects them to the broader machinery of power that shielded Epstein for decades. We’ll revisit the timeline from his first arrests through his 2008 plea deal, and into the re-investigations that followed his 2019 death in federal custody. And we won’t stop there—we’ll look closely at the current state of affairs: the closed probes, the lingering co-conspirators, the civil suits, and the glaring gaps in accountability.

What makes The Coverup Chronicles different is that we’re not here to sensationalize the story—we’re here to document the ongoing concealment of it. This isn’t just about reliving Epstein’s crimes. It’s about following the networks that enabled them, protected him, and continue to obscure the truth. If you want an honest look at what’s still being hidden—by whom, and why—this is the podcast that pulls those threads.


And I should know—I’ve spent over six years uncovering every dark corner of this case. My name is Bobby Capucci, and I’ve dedicated those same six years  exposing the truth about Epstein and the powerful figures who enabled him. From on-the-ground investigations at Epstein’s Zorro Ranch, where I spoke with insiders, to national appearances on Tucker Carlson, I’ve followed this story farther than most are willing to go.


Who helped Epstein build his empire? Who protected him? And who is still pulling the strings? The answers lie in the shadows of Jeffrey Epstein's criminal empire.  .

This is the truth they don’t want you to hear. And I’m here to make sure you do.

structure.rationale: There is a loose but identifiable throughline moving from UK airport trafficking claims to French investigations, anchored by article citations, but the episode frequently digresses into editorial rants that disrupt pacing.

relevance.rationale: Directly tied to active 2025-2026 Epstein file releases, named ongoing UK and French investigations, Gordon Brown's public statement, and Essex Police's current assessment — all highly timely hooks.

clipability.rationale: Several punchy standalone moments — particularly the 'a child can't be a prostitute' argument and the Gordon Brown flight-log revelation — work without much setup, though profanity and tangents limit universal usability.

card.tagline: Scotland Yard knew. France knew. So why did it take this long?
card.description: New UK and French investigations into Epstein's trafficking routes through Stanstead Airport prompt a sharp breakdown of what authorities ignored for decades.
card.reason: Because this episode covers the international Epstein fallout unfolding right now

scored.description:
🎙️ 97.9 WJLB – Detroit’s #1 Station for Hip-Hop & R&B… Now On Demand! 

Tap in with the voices of the D! The culture, the heat, and the realest convos are now just a play button away.

Catch the best of The Breakfast Club, Way Up with Angela Yee, and 97.9’s own Big Dawgg Blast, Chris Carsyn, and UKnowCell as they bring you unfiltered interviews, wild moments, and nonstop vibes straight from the heart of the city.

From Charlamagne’s iconic Donkey of the Day, to Angela Yee’s Rumor Report, and your favorite guilty pleasure, Tell Me a Secret…you’ll laugh and hit replay again, and again. 🎧

Missed it live? No worries! Stream the hottest highlights, exclusive drops, and can't-miss moments. Follow us and bring the 97.9 WJLB experience with you!

scored.description: Introducing where it all starts, hosted by two VERY normal teenage girls who talk about dream lives, family experiences, expressing feelings and differences in a more of a comedic way. Like any other teenagers, they talk about how tiktok has influenced their lives ESPECIALLY during corona and how they are trying to keep their mind off the pandemic. They end it off with a very memorable quote, one that'll surely have an impact on your day to day life.

structure.rationale: The episode moves through distinct topics (SCOTUS campaign finance, Democratic Socialist primaries, housing bill) but transitions are loose and conversational, with tangents about smoke-filled rooms and local radio that interrupt the throughline.

relevance.rationale: The episode directly covers multiple specific Supreme Court end-of-term rulings — birthright citizenship, trans athletes, and campaign finance — plus live primary results and a White House housing bill standoff, all pegged to a single news-dense day.

clipability.rationale: Karl Rove's extended quote on Citizens United and party-strengthening is a genuinely self-contained, quotable moment, and the seven-percent-turnout observation on Democratic Socialists works as a standalone hook, though most segments require surrounding context.

card.tagline: SCOTUS just blew up campaign finance rules — what it means for your elections
card.description: Tom Sullivan breaks down the Supreme Court's final-day rulings on campaign coordination limits, trans athletes, and birthright citizenship, arguing the decisions defy simple partisan framing.
card.reason: Because the Supreme Court just handed down its most consequential term-ending rulings

structure.rationale: Follows a consistent radio sports-update format with clear game previews, coach quotes, and a rundown of other scheduled games, though the long scoreboard-style listing of matchups creates a dense, hard-to-navigate middle section.

relevance.rationale: Covers local Iowa high school baseball and softball schedules and stats with no connection to broader news or political events, placing it firmly in hyper-local sports rather than anything a News/Politics listener would seek out.

clipability.rationale: Coach soundbites (e.g., 'I'm not going to tell you because I don't want anybody else to know') offer occasional standalone moments, but most content is stat-heavy play-preview material that requires local context to land.

scored.description: Philippine Politics Now is your essential guide to the complex, fast-paced, and ever-evolving world of Philippine governance, policy, and power dynamics. Everyday, we pull back the curtain on the headlines that matter, breaking down intense political debates, legislative battles, and grassroots movements into clear, actionable insights. Featuring candid interviews with lawmakers, journalists, academics, and key newsmakers, we explore how policy decisions made in Manila echo across the archipelago and the global stage. Tune in to understand the why behind the what in Philippine politics today.
