structure.rationale: The episode moves loosely from semiconductor volatility to SpaceX to Supreme Court rulings to Trump gas price criticism with minimal signposting or deliberate transitions between segments.

relevance.rationale: The episode touches on timely current events including a Supreme Court birthright citizenship ruling, Title IX sports decision, and Trump's gas price demands, but these are secondary to market commentary on semiconductors and SpaceX.

clipability.rationale: The Trump gas station rant and the Title IX swim meet anecdote are both self-contained enough to stand alone, though the financial commentary requires more market context to land.

card.tagline: Gary Kaltbaum calls Trump a socialist over gas station price demands
card.description: Market analyst Gary Kaltbaum covers wild semiconductor swings and SpaceX volatility, then unloads on Trump's gas price ultimatum and Republican hypocrisy.
card.reason: Because this episode covers Trump's gas price demands and today's Supreme Court rulings

scored.description:
Why are so many Minnesota high school graduates unable to read or do math at grade level? Does Tim Walz actually think he's going to be the next president of the United States? And why does he keep swearing so much? 
 
These are the kinds of hard-hitting questions we get into every week on the American Experiment Podcast, where we unpack the week's biggest stories, interview Minnesota's movers and shakers, and "stop the tape" on clips of our state's most ridiculous elected officials. 
 
New episodes drop every Tuesday afternoon and are available on every major podcasting platform.

structure.rationale: The episode moves through distinct story segments with a clear conversational throughline, but transitions are informal and pacing is uneven, with tangents (the dog park drama) disrupting the policy focus before a guest interview segment begins.

relevance.rationale: The episode covers specific, timely Minnesota policy issues — SNAP penalties, a federal lawsuit, school budget negotiations, and a state paid leave program going dark — but the scope is largely state-level and the SNAP headline is the sharpest national hook.

clipability.rationale: The school board member's Facebook post about 'pissing on white corpses' and the observation that Minnesota's paid leave program has gone radio silent since April are genuinely self-contained, reaction-worthy moments, though most segments require at least minimal setup.

card.tagline: Minnesota owes $130M in SNAP penalties — and won't hand over the records
card.description: Two hosts unpack Minnesota's federal SNAP lawsuit, a billion-dollar school district budget crisis, and a state paid leave program that's mysteriously stopped reporting numbers.
card.reason: Because this episode tracks a live federal-state standoff over welfare fraud data

scored.feedTitle: Podmastery: podcasting insights and advice for indie creators
scored.description:
Are you a podcaster who's looking to improve and grow your podcast? You're in the right place. Together, we'll help you attain 'podmastery'. 
I'm Neal Veglio, a veteran podcaster who first started dabbling with RSS in the early 2000s.
Podcasting is such a complex medium, with so many factors that can impact your success. It's my goal to cover all these topics with you, and help you maximise the results you're getting from your efforts.
And while my shows now enjoy thousands of listeners every single week, it wasn't always easy. 
When I took a career break from radio for a few years in 2007, I had to learn how to build audience without the lift of an FM frequency. 
I learned a lot from that experience.
I now help other podcasters to achieve their goals.
Each episode, I'll be offering you some insights into what I've done and what I've helped my  podcasting agency clients to do with all our podcasts in the hope we can help YOU increase your podcast's chances of becoming more successful! 
And ensuring you can avoid the dreaded 'podfade'!
Take one step closer to Podmastery. Click follow in your favourite podcast app, and let's grow together.

Anyway, what makes this tech different from the MP4 pass-through standard that Libsyn have bragged on providing for years is that this tech streams in chunks. transcript.scoringWindow: Alexander Pool in Princeton to open this week. Written by Bill Frescos, This article is being read by an automated voice brought to you by Everlett. Alexander Pool and Princeton will officially open Wednesday, July first, after repairs delayed the start of the summer season. The Princeton Park District provided an update after Alexander Pool was closed last month due to a major mechanical failure with their filtration pump and motor. The delay came while the park district was awaiting the delivery of the equipment. The equipment came in this week, and the park district officials announced the reopening Tuesday, thanking maintenance staff for completing the necessary repairs and preparing the pool for use. We appreciate everyone's patience and understanding while navigating the pool repairs, the park district said in a statement. The reopening of the pool extended open swim hours at the Princeton Metro Center will return to normal beginning Wednesday. Because of the delayed opening, Discounted pool pass pricing will also be available as lifeguard availability allows. The city plans to keep the pool open later into the season to help make up for lost time. Information regarding aquatats and group swim lessons will be announced at a later date. For updates and up to date schedules, visit the Park District's website or call eight one five eight seven two zero eight four zero

clipability.rationale: Every sentence is purely informational and context-dependent, with no quotable, standalone moments that would function as a compelling preview clip.

card.tagline: Princeton's Alexander Pool finally reopens after mechanical failure delayed summer season
card.description: After a filtration pump failure shut down Alexander Pool for weeks, Princeton Park District announces a July 1 reopening with discounted passes and extended season hours.
card.reason: Because you follow local Illinois Valley community updates and municipal news

scored.description: Russia Decoded explains how the Kremlin shapes its narrative of current domestic and international events through tightly curated state news broadcasts. Co-hosts Andy Kuchins, a senior fellow at CFTNI and adjunct professor at Johns Hopkins SAIS in Washington, and Chris Monday, a professor of economics at Dongseo University in Busan, South Korea, are veteran observers of Soviet and Russian media. Kuchins and Monday frame their discussions around the weekly Russian news round-up program Vesti Nedeli (“News of the Week”), broadcast on Russia’s Channel 2 on Sunday evenings. Viewed through the right lens, Vesti Nedeli offers a uniquely revealing perspective on how Vladimir Putin and Russian elites understand the world, and how they seek to influence it.

structure.rationale: The discussion meanders between Budanov speculation, Lukashenko, the United Russia congress, and 2011 historical parallels without clear segmentation or signposting, making it feel like an unstructured conversation rather than a produced episode.

relevance.rationale: The episode engages directly with current Russia-Ukraine war dynamics, Zelensky's threats to Lukashenko, Belarus radar sites, and upcoming Russian parliamentary elections — all timely, though the analysis is conversational rather than sharply news-driven.

clipability.rationale: Most moments require surrounding context to land — the Zelensky-Lukashenko radar threat is the strongest standalone hook, but it's buried in hedged speculation and crosstalk that would need heavy editing to work as a preview clip.

card.tagline: Zelensky threatens Belarus, and Russia's sham elections may backfire on Putin
card.description: Two Russia analysts decode Zelensky's strike threat against Belarusian radar sites and why September's Duma elections could embarrass the Kremlin with a humiliatingly low turnout.
card.reason: Because this episode unpacks the week's most consequential Russia-Belarus escalation

structure.rationale: The episode meanders through a Huntress ethics debate before pivoting abruptly to the FIFA story, with loose roundtable pacing and no clear signposting or deliberate segment transitions.

relevance.rationale: The episode covers infosec industry drama and a FIFA World Cup vulnerability — niche cybersecurity topics with no meaningful connection to current news or political events a News/Politics listener would seek out.

clipability.rationale: The cold-war spy analogy for threat actor communications and the 'my browser said so' FIFA client-side RBAC quip are genuinely quotable, but most moments require surrounding context to land.

card.tagline: Should cybersecurity firms pay hackers for intel? The ethics debate gets messy.
card.description: Huntress whistleblower drama sparks a roundtable on threat intel ethics, dark web access, and how a FIFA World Cup hack nearly rick-rolled a billion viewers.
card.reason: Because this episode debates where ethical hacking ends and criminal conduct begins

scored.description:
Free speech, culture, censorship, Big Tech, and the conversations shaping the future are all colliding in real time, and most people are only seeing a small piece of the picture. The Jeff Dornik Show brings together voices from across politics, business, health, faith, and culture to have real conversations that cut through narratives and get to the truth.

Hosted by Jeff Dornik, founder and CEO of Pickax, this show is built on one premise: the most important ideas are often the ones that are ignored, suppressed, or misunderstood. Through in-depth interviews and direct discussions, Jeff explores the forces influencing our world, from political agendas and cultural shifts to personal health, entrepreneurship, and the battle over free expression in the digital age.

This is not a scripted or controlled environment. Guests are free to speak, ideas are challenged openly, and conversations go where they need to go. Whether it is politics, business, faith, health, or culture, the goal is simple: honest dialogue without filters.

If you are looking for real conversations, diverse perspectives, and a deeper understanding of what is actually happening beneath the surface, this show is for you.

structure.rationale: The conversation meanders from voter ID to Epstein blackmail theories to California registration data without clear segmentation, and the transcript is interrupted by ad breaks and a sinus-medicine parody spot that disrupt any throughline.

relevance.rationale: The episode addresses specific claims about California's RAVBM program, the 2021 Newsom recall, and ongoing election integrity debates, giving it timely political hooks, though the published date of 2026-06-30 makes some referenced events feel dated.

clipability.rationale: The Newsom recall prediction anecdote ('sixty-five thirty-five, almost exactly spot on') is the one genuinely self-contained moment, but most exchanges require the surrounding conspiratorial framing to land.

card.tagline: Did California engineer a permanent 65-35 split — and does anyone care?
card.description: Jeff Dornik argues California's independent-voter majority should scramble election results but never does, pointing to structural rigging and both-party complicity.
card.reason: Because this episode digs into California's election math and why it never changes

relevance.rationale: Covers specific, timely UFO disclosure developments tied to the Trump administration, named officials like Stephen Miller and Lou Elizondo, and ongoing war.gov document releases, though the show's fringe subject matter limits its News/Politics ceiling.

clipability.rationale: Several genuinely self-contained, quotable moments — particularly the 'it's not us, it's the CIA' exchange and the 'Chinese are three or four steps ahead' warning — work as standalone hooks without requiring surrounding context.

card.tagline: The U.S. government is asking reporters what UFO files to release next

card.reason: Because this episode covers an active government transparency fight unfolding right now

scored.description:
NaturalQueen77 TV is a true crime podcast dedicated to the cases the mainstream forgot. While the biggest names in crime get documentaries and bestsellers, countless victims and unsolved mysteries slip through the cracks. This show pulls them back into the light.

Each episode dives deep into lesser-known murders, disappearances, and cold cases — examining the evidence, the suspects, and the systemic failures that left these cases gathering dust. And when a well-known case has more to the story than people think, we cover that too.

From small-town tragedies to overlooked urban cases to infamous crimes that still have unanswered questions, NaturalQueen77 TV tells the stories that deserve airtime.
New episodes drop weekly. Subscribe and follow on social media to join the conversation.

transcript.scoringWindow: County and the surrounding area. Our experienced staff provides a range of inpatient and outpatient services available to residents of our community. To learn more about the services provided at Marshall Medical Center, visit MauryRegional.com/MMC. Foster Families Honored (MSM) Foster families from across Middle Tennessee walked the red carpet at the Franklin Theater this month at an annual Youth Villages event to celebrate and honor their dedication to the children in their homes. Maury County parents were among those who were honored. Five Years of Service Award recipients from Maury County included Donald and Dawn Campbell. of Spring Hill, and James and Amy Mayberry, of Columbia. “Foster parents play such a critical, often thankless, role in our community, opening up their homes and their lives to children as they navigate separation from their homes,” said Brittany Farrar, senior executive director of Middle Tennessee at Youth Villages. “Our team tries to ensure they feel supported, appreciated and part of a community throughout the year, and this event is a big part of that.” Youth Villages, now in its 40th year, is one of Tennessee’s largest foster care providers. The organization provides families with additional training and therapeutic support to ensure children with increased emotional or behavioral needs who enter the child welfare system have homes prepared to care for them. “We don’t foster because we expect any type of recognition,” said Travis VanZant, Foster Parent of the Year. “We do it because we’re passionate about it, and we love seeing the kids grow while in our care and being part of a family [that is] reuniting. But, we appreciate the acknowledgment and validation that we’re making a difference in the kids’ lives.” For more information on Youth Villages’ foster care program, visit youthvillages.org/foster. Historic Marker Dedication (Press Release) The City of Columbia recently unveiled a historical marker commemorating the Maury County Jail Fire of 1977 during a ceremony held on the 49th anniversary of the tragedy, preserving the history of one of the most significant events in the community's past through the City's Historical Marker Program. On June 26, 1977, a deadly fire broke out inside the Maury County Jail, claiming the lives of 42 people, including inmates and visitors. The fire remains the deadliest jail fire in Tennessee history and the second deadliest in the nation. The tragedy began when padding inside a jail cell was set ablaze, causing smoke to rapidly spread throughout the building. Despite heroic efforts by local law enforcement officers, firefighters, emergency personnel, and volunteers to rescue those trapped inside, many victims were unable to escape in the first critical moments of the fire. The disaster prompted significant changes in jail design and construction standards across the country, helping improve safety measures in correctional facilities nationwide. “This marker serves as a permanent reminder of a tragedy that forever changed our community and influenced correctional facility safety across the nation,” said Columbia Mayor Chaz Molder. “While we cannot undo the pain experienced by so many families, we can ensure that the lives lost are never forgotten. Preserving and sharing these stories is an important part of remembering our past and educating future generations.” Following the fire, the building was restored and continued to serve as the Maury County Jail until 1998. In 2001, it was converted into the Maury County Archives, and in January 2025, the facility was renovated, expanded, and rededicated, continuing its role as a place where local history is preserved and shared. City Manager Tony Massey, who established the City of Columbia Historical Marker Program with the assistance of the late Bob Duncan, said the program was created to preserve the people, places, and events that have shaped Columbia's history. “As a young man living in Columbia I recall the tragedy," said Massey. “The Maury County Jail Fire left an indelible mark on Columbia, and it is important that future generations understand both the tragedy itself and the lasting impact it had on public safety throughout the country. Through our Historical Marker Program, we are committed to preserving the stories that define Columbia’s heritage.” Since its creation, the City of Columbia Historical Marker Program has recognized 14 sites of local historical significance, including the Bethel Hotel/Princess Theatre, Union Station Train Depot, Columbia Fire & Rescue, and Fairview Park with additional markers currently in development. The program complements historical and commemorative markers installed by the Tennessee Historical Commission, Tennessee Civil War Trails, and other local organizations, creating a lasting record of the people, places, and events that have shaped Columbia while preserving those stories for future generations. Blood Donors Needed (Press Release) Local hospitals are using blood faster than donations are coming in. That’s why Blood Assurance is offering $40 e-gift cards to all donors who give between June 29 and July 7. The call for donors comes as the July Fourth holiday approaches, a time when hospitals must be ready for trauma patients while blood donations are often slow because of travel, vacations and holiday plans. Blood Assurance needs donors of all blood types. The nonprofit has had several recent days with less than a one-day supply of blood, platelets and plasma. A two- to three-day supply is needed to more comfortably meet the needs of local hospitals and patients. Blood Assurance is the primary supplier for most of the hospitals in Middle Tennessee, so donations help local patients. Donors will be able to choose their e-gift card from a list of participating retailers, including gas stations and grocery stores. Eligible type O donors can earn even more through Blood Assurance’s Hero Club, including a $50 gift card for double red cell donations. That means O donors could receive up to $90 for one double red cell donation during the June 29-July 7 promotion. O-negative blood can be used for emergency transfusions when there is no time to determine a patient’s blood type, and O-positive blood is the most common blood type and is heavily used by hospitals. Donors must sign up for the Hero Club at bloodassurance.org/blood-donation-education/types-of-donations/hero-club/. After July 7, donors will receive a $25 e-gift card for donating. Donors can schedule an appointment at bloodassurance.org, call 800-962-0628, or text BAGIVE to 999777. Columbia State Opens New Exhibition (MSM) After exhibiting the curated works of local artists for two months, the Pryor Art Gallery at Columbia State Community College will turn the spotlight on works from its permanent collection. “Rhythm & Form: Selections from the Permanent Collection” features sculptures gifted from the estate of David Herbert Goerss, along with abstract paintings from the collection. When the exhibition is over, the works now collected in the Pryor Gallery will be redistributed back throughout the campuses; for now, “Rhythm & Form” offers visitors the chance to view them all in one place. Columbia State Community College has a permanent collection of art with over 200 pieces, displayed across Columbia State’s five campuses. This collection is made possible by the generosity of Pryor’s donors and the support of the Columbia State Foundation. “We are so fortunate to have a permanent collection that is full of unique and impactful works of art,” said Tori Zemer, the new director of the Gallery. “Whether they inspire a burst of energy or a moment of respite, these pieces serve our student body year-round. It is a pleasure to be able to present them in a way that makes them accessible for the public to enjoy as well.” The exhibit, which is free and open to the public, will run from now to July 24. The gallery is open from 8 a.m.-7 p.m. from Monday through Thursday, and from 8 a.m.-4 p.m. on Friday. America 250th Celebration (Press Release) In celebration of America’s 250th anniversary, the City of Columbia is recognizing the community’s rich heritage and the valuable contributions of individuals who helped shape its history. As part of the America 250 commemoration, the City has launched Columbia250USA.com, a dedicated microsite designed to share information about local events and highlight some of the people who have left a lasting impact on Columbia. Central to the site is a special feature recognizing 26 of Columbia’s most influential figures, referred to as the "Architects of History." These leaders, legends, and visionaries represent the spirit and legacy of Columbia across generations. To further recognize these individuals, the City has brought their stories to the heart of downtown. Decorative banners featuring the 26 historical figures are now displayed throughout the downtown area, creating a visual tribute that connects residents and visitors to the city's past. The website includes brief biographies of each honoree, offering insight into their contributions and lasting influence. The historical content was generously provided by the Maury County Archives and the African American Heritage Society of Maury County. Columbia Mayor Chaz Molder emphasized the importance of recognizing Columbia's unique role in the nation's story. "This initiative is about more than marking a milestone; it's about recognizing the people who built our community,” said Molder. "It's about ensuring that their stories continue to be told and inspire future generations. As we recognize America's 250th anniversary throughout 2026, Columbia is proud to celebrate our shared history while looking ahead to the future we are building together." The City encourages residents and visitors to explore the stories of Columbia's Architects of History by visiting Columbia250USA.com. The America 250 Independence Day weekend celebration will kick off on Friday, July 3, as the City of Columbia partners with Columbia Main Street for a special USA 250th themed First Fridays event in downtown Columbia from 5:00 p.m. to 8:00 p.m. The patriotic celebration will feature live music from the Main Stage, musicians and buskers performing throughout downtown, and a family-friendly Game Zone for all ages. Attendees are encouraged to show their patriotic spirit by wearing red, white, and blue, stars and stripes, patriotic t-shirts, or festive Americana-inspired attire. Residents and visitors alike are invited to join the community in celebrating Columbia's history, America's heritage, and the people whose contributions continue to shape the city today. For more information about Columbia's America 250 initiatives and upcoming events, visit Columbia250USA.com. And now, Your Hometown Memorials, Sponsored by Oakes & Nichols Funeral Home… David Houston Harris, 72, a lifelong resident of Columbia, passed away peacefully at his home on Thursday, June 25, 2026. Graveside services will be conducted on Sunday, July 12, 2026, at 2:00 p.m. at Pleasant Mount Cemetery. Online condolences may be extended to the family at www.oakesandnichols.com. Williamson Taskforce Recommends Location for Courthouse (WilliamsonScene) The Williamson County Special Courthouse Task Force is recommending Franklin’s H.G. Hill property as the location for the next county courthouse. The 16-member task force, formed earlier this year and made up of numerous government leaders — including county commissioners, judges, prosecutors, and defense attorneys — voted on the recommendation during its June 22 meeting, with only two “no” votes from Franklin Mayor Ken Moore and Franklin City Administrator Eric Stuckey. “I think that it's a little bit premature to approve that property, personally — I don't know that there's been an adequate investigation as far as the building type and infrastructure, and so on, that's there,” Moore said, adding, “I'm still not completely convinced that we evaluate all the other potential sites.” Several members pushed back on that argument, including Williamson County Circuit Judge Deana Hood. “We've had a task force, we've had people engaged, people coming to the courthouse — we have investigated,” Hood said. “There is not another property known to us at this point in time, eight months after we put the contract on the H.G. Hill property, that we have found to be a suitable place to put the courthouse, so I respectfully disagree, in that, I believe that this committee has looked ... I firmly believe that we need a new courthouse. So, if not H.G. Hill, then where?” The $17.5 million Columbia Avenue property has stood empty for half a decade after several businesses exited the now-demolished shopping center. Under the ongoing discussions, a new courthouse could

structure.rationale: The segment is largely unscripted and reactive — DeSantis is processing breaking news in real time, resulting in loose, meandering commentary without clear signposting or a defined close.

relevance.rationale: Directly addresses a same-day Supreme Court ruling on birthright citizenship with specific legal and constitutional analysis tied to Trump's executive order.

clipability.rationale: The Dred Scott historical framing and the 'showed up in France' analogy are self-contained and punchy enough to stand alone, though most of the segment requires surrounding context to land fully.

card.tagline: DeSantis reacts live as SCOTUS upholds birthright citizenship in 6-3 ruling
card.description: DeSantis breaks down the same-day Supreme Court decision, arguing the Fourteenth Amendment was never meant to cover birth tourism or illegal immigration.
card.reason: Because the Supreme Court just handed down a major ruling on birthright citizenship

structure.rationale: The episode has a clear throughline moving from the Fender/guitar IP case into broader dupe culture with an identifiable mid-roll break and return, though ad interruptions disrupt pacing and the excerpt shows some meandering transitions.

relevance.rationale: The episode covers dupe culture and IP law as a cultural/consumer trend rather than a timely political or news event, making it loosely relevant to current discourse but lacking a sharp news hook.

clipability.rationale: Several self-contained moments work well as standalone clips — the 'music is meant to be free' reflection, the dupe.com explainer, and the UGG boot jury ruling summary each land without requiring heavy setup.

card.tagline: Fender sued over guitar shapes — and now everything is a dupe
card.description: From Birkin bag knockoffs to guitar body shapes, Today Explained investigates how dupe culture went mainstream and why IP law can't keep up.
card.reason: Because this episode unpacks the legal and cultural battle over who owns a shape

scored.description:
Welcome to the New Era Woman Podcast—
where purpose meets leadership and faith in real life.

This is a space for women who are healing, building, and becoming aligned. 

Each episode offers real conversations, biblical wisdom, and practical strategies to help you strengthen your inner world, hear God with confidence, and walk in peace, love, and emotional steadiness.

Whether you’re new to faith, returning to God, or seeking deeper discipleship, you belong here. 

Visit www.NewEraWoman.org and follow @newerawomanpodcast on IG to stay connected.

Follow us on Instagram at @newerawomanpodcast

Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/the-new-era-woman-network--4771294/support.

structure.rationale: The episode is largely stream-of-consciousness with a loose pivot to a program promotion at the end, and the host herself acknowledges going off-script mid-episode.

relevance.rationale: This episode covers personal wellness and legacy themes with no connection to current news or political events.

clipability.rationale: Most moments are deeply embedded in personal narrative and require surrounding context; the closing scripted line about legacy is the only near-standalone excerpt.

card.tagline: Coach Michelle gets vulnerable about obesity, confidence, and the wellness legacy you leave
card.description: A registered nurse and coach reflects on childhood food culture, using education as a body-image shield, and setting a physical wellness goal for July.
card.reason: Because this episode speaks directly to women rethinking their health legacy

relevance.rationale: The episode covers timely South African business and economic stories — fuel price relief, a landmark Competition Commission ruling against major banks, Zimbabwe's energy infrastructure, and AI-driven mineral exploration — but the connections to the headline topics (fuel prices, shutdown) are thin in this excerpt.

clipability.rationale: The Rob Rose exchange on the Competition Commission overreach contains a few quotable moments, but most segments require significant context about South African institutions, named individuals, and ongoing cases to land with a general listener.

card.tagline: South Africa's banks just won a decade-long fight — what it means for business
card.description: A landmark Competition Commission defeat, fuel price relief, Zimbabwe's floating solar gamble, and AI rewriting Africa's mineral maps — all in one episode.
card.reason: Because this episode unpacks a major regulatory ruling reshaping South African banking

scored.description: Real America’s Voice: Real News, Honest Views - Dive into a world of unfiltered news, in-depth analysis, and thought-provoking discussions featuring some of your favorite personalities. At Real America’s Voice, we cover a wide array of topics—politics, culture, and current events—through the lens of American values and freedom. We pride ourselves on delivering honest content that challenges mainstream narratives. Our passionate hosts are experts and commentators who offer unique insights on the issues that matter most to you. Whether you start your day with American Sunrise, follow breaking news in the War Room with Steve Bannon, gain political insights from Charlie Kirk, explore cultural commentary with Jack Posobiec, or tune-in for the latest with investigative reporter John Solomon and Just The News, you’ll find all your favorite programs on RAV right here on the RAV podcast, including coverage of our live events featured on the network. Join the Real America’s Voice community to stay informed, engaged, and inspired. Subscribe now and never miss an episode!

card.tagline: Senator Rick Scott reacts live to SCOTUS striking down Trump's birthright ban

scored.feedTitle: The Daily Mojo with Brad Staggs
scored.description:
The Daily Mojo with Brad Staggs and Producer Ron Phillips is your first look each weekday, at the news, politics, faith, and absurdity that America is fast becoming. Brad and Producer Ron Phillips lens it all through a filter of truth, cynicism, and laughter (that’s getting tougher by the minute).

Our core value – We The People are at the heart of these United States and WE need to wrest our power back from the DC swamp dwellers. If you believe in freedom, personal responsibility, and individual rights, you may have found soulmates.

The Daily Mojo is life in the Conservatarian lane each weekday from 8-10a ET at TheDailyMojo.com, and wherever you get your favorite podcasts! Follow @RealBradStaggs @RealRonPhillips

Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/the-daily-mojo-with-brad-staggs--3085897/support.

structure.rationale: Conversation meanders between topics without clear segmentation, transitions are abrupt, and the discussion frequently loses its thread mid-sentence, reflecting an unscripted radio interview format with minimal editorial shaping.

relevance.rationale: Episode directly addresses same-day Supreme Court rulings on Title IX transgender policy and campaign finance coordination, with Louisiana's AG providing state-level legal analysis of decisions issued the same day.

clipability.rationale: A few quotable moments exist — particularly Murrill's plain-language summary of the Title IX ruling — but most exchanges require surrounding context to land, and the crosstalk-heavy format makes clean 30-45 second excerpts difficult to isolate.

card.tagline: Louisiana's AG breaks down the Supreme Court's Title IX and campaign finance rulings
card.description: Attorney General Liz Murrill explains what the Court's same-day Title IX and PAC coordination decisions actually mean for Louisiana law and the fights still ahead.
card.reason: Because this episode covers landmark Supreme Court rulings handed down this week

scored.description:
Join the journey with David Knight, a true veteran in the realm of news and analysis, and become part of a community that values historical context, critical think, and spiritual insight in navigating the challenges of our era.  
LIVE every weekday morning at 9am Eastern where you'll find an honest, fresh perspective, not partisan cheerleading.  WIth a background as an electrical engineer, David is focused on the interplay of technology & liberty from a Christian perspective.

David has had decades of experience in the trenches of politics as an activist, organizer, candidate, reporter, analyst, and news anchor









Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/the-real-david-knight-show--5282736/support.

structure.rationale: The episode meanders from energy codes to Monsanto to a lengthy Blockbuster video tangent with no clear segmentation or signposting, making it difficult to follow a coherent throughline.

relevance.rationale: Covers specific current developments including the Supreme Court's Monsanto/Roundup ruling, Trump administration energy code rollbacks, and EPA regulatory policy, though the episode drifts into extended personal anecdotes about 1990s video stores.

clipability.rationale: A few punchy standalone lines exist (the 'casting out a demon' analogy, the flat-tax comparison for building codes) but most moments require surrounding context or trail off into digressions before landing cleanly.

card.tagline: Supreme Court shields Monsanto while Trump subsidizes glyphosate — who's protecting you?
card.description: Knight argues the Monsanto ruling, Trump's EPA stance, and energy code rollbacks reveal a bipartisan pattern of federal power protecting corporate interests over consumers.
card.reason: Because this episode covers the Supreme Court's biggest corporate liability ruling of the term

scored.description: Welcome to Furniture Industry News the premier podcast dedicated to executives navigating the ever-evolving world of furniture. In a landscape where the latest trends, market shifts, and innovative technologies can redefine success overnight, our podcast stands as your authoritative source for everything you need to stay ahead. Tune in to stay informed, inspired, and in step with the future of furniture. Furniture Industry News – where leaders come to learn, share, and shape the future of the furniture world.

latestEpisode.title: Tariff Money Is on the Table and the Clock Is Ticking

transcript.scoringWindow: Welcome to Furniture Industry News. Today is Tuesday, June 30, 2026, and thanks for spending a few minutes with us. This week is really about one reading a consumer who hasn't gone away but has gotten a lot more careful. Spending looks steady on the surface, but underneath it, people are buying fewer things and weighing every dollar. We'll also dig into a tariff story that matters more for our industry than almost anyone else's. A real bright spot out of Target's home business and what the latest Amazon sales event tells us about how people are shopping right now. Let's get into it. Let's start with the consumer, because everything else flows from here. New numbers from the research firm Surkana show that overall retail spending in May rose about 1.3% compared with a year ago. On its face, that sounds healthy. But here's to catch While the dollars went up, the actual number of items people bought fell by about one and a half percent. So people are spending a little more money on fewer things, and most of that growth is simply higher prices, not stronger demand. Now zoom into the discretionary category, the bucket that includes home goods and furnishings. Revenue there inched up just over 1%, but unit sales dropped more than 4%. That gap is the real story. When money gets tight, people don't stop spending altogether. They stop buying the things they can put off. And a new sofa or bedroom set is one of the easiest purchases in any household to postpone. One of Serkana's advisors, Marshall Cohen, made a point I think every operator should sit with, he said. Resilience in consumer spending is not automatic. It has to be activated. That's the whole game right now. The customer is engaged and intentional. But they need a real reason to buy today, whether that's smart financing, a genuine promotion or a sense of urgency you create on the floor. The mood data backs this up. The University of Michigan's closely watched sentiment index actually rose in June, climbing to 49.5 from 44.8 in May and snapping three straight months of decline. The encouraging piece is that the part of the survey measuring expectations for the Future jumped about 15%, helped along by easing gas prices and cooling worries about overseas conflict. But, and this is important, confidence is still running nearly 30% below where it was a year ago. And when researchers asked people what worried them more in the year ahead inflation or unemployment? 36% Pointed to inflation, the highest share in well over a year, while only 7% named unemployment. So treat the rebound as fragile. People feel a little better, but they're still bracing for higher prices. And that keeps big ticket purchases like ours under pressure. Now for a genuine bright spot. Target's home business just grew for the first time in two years. Its home furnishings and decor segment rose about 6/10 of a percent in the most recent quarter to roughly $3.24 billion. That's a small gain, but it broke a streak of eight straight quarters of year over year declines in a category Target used to dominate. To put the slump in perspective, the company hasn't grown home sales on an annual basis since 2021, and last fiscal year alone its home sales fell by more than a billion dollars. So how are they turning it around? They're swapping out nearly three quarters of their decorative accessories, refreshing kids Home in bedding next, and rolling out dedicated shop and shop space for their strongest private label across 200 stores. Why does this matter to us? Because Target competes for the very same home goods dollar we do. Even a small gain signals tougher competition ahead. And their playbook clearing out tired assortments and concentrating behind your best brands is worth what size operation you run under a new chief executive. This is a multi year rebuild, so expect a more aggressive Target in home through 2027. Now to the story I'd circle for everyone in our industry. Tariffs there's good news and a serious catch the good news is that furniture importers just got another shot at recovering tariffs they paid under an emergency powers. Program that the courts struck down. As of late June, U.S. customs widened its refund system to accept a new category of import entries that couldn't be filed before. For furniture companies, many of whom rely heavily on imported product and components, that can mean real cash coming back. But it's procedural and deadline driven, so the refunds will go to the companies that move first. The practical advice is to pull your import records now, figure out which shipments newly qualify and make sure someone actually owns those filings before the deadlines pass. Don't assume your customs broker is already on it. Here's the catch, and it's a big one. You've probably seen that the warehouse clubs Walmart, Costco and BJ's are planning to take the tariffs they recover and turn them into lower prices for shoppers. That sounds great until you realize furniture mostly can't follow them there. The tariffs that hit our industry hardest a 25% duty on upholstered furniture and kitchen cabinets and a separate 25% duty on Chinese made furniture were not the ones the courts threw out. They're still on the books. On top of that, the government opened a new trade investigation in March, covering nearly every major furniture exporting country from China and Vietnam to India and Mexico. So while a big box can advertise a tariff refund price cut, most furniture sellers can't. And that could leave our dealers at a disadvantage as shoppers come to expect those deals. Even William Sonoma, one of the biggest names in home furnishings, has said it isn't counting on refunds in its planning this year, with its finance chief admitting it's impossible to say where tariffs will ultimately land. The home furnishings association is urging members to file their claims as soon as possible because the longer you wait, the harder those refunds get to recover the bottom line. File early, know your deadlines, and don't build your pricing for the rest of the year around refund money you may never see. Let me close with the latest read on how people are actually shopping, and it ties everything together. Amazon's big summer sales event just wrapped, and it broke records even as the average shopping cart got lighter. According to Adobe, shoppers spent $26.4 billion online over the four days, up more than 9% from last year, so strong that Adobe says summer is starting to rival the winter holidays as a shopping season. Encouragingly for us, furniture and bedding sales jumped about 55% over a typical June day during the event. But here's the other side. A second firm numerator found the average order size actually fell about 11% from last year to just under $48, and the average household spend about 8% less. Both of those things can be true at once. More people are shopping, but each one is spending a little less. That smaller, more careful basket is the same story we've heard all episode people are engaged but cautious, and they're hunting for value. In fact, when you look at where shoppers splurged on premium products, home and garden was one of the few categories that went the other way, with buyers trading down rather than up. The lesson is that event driven urgency still works. Furniture moved when the deals were strong, but the offer has to feel like real value before people act. One more signal worth your attention Adobe found that shopping traffic coming from AI assistants jumped almost 90% during the event. And this is the striking part. It converted 40% better than other channels. Just a year ago, that same AI traffic actually converted worse than average. That's a fast turnaround, and it tells you AI is quickly becoming a real path to purchase, not just a research tool. It's worth making sure your products and your stores show up well when a shopper asks an AI assistant for a recommendation. So that's the rundown for this week. The through line is a consumer who's engaged but careful, a tariff picture that's tougher for furniture than for the big boxes, and a couple of genuine bright spots in Target's home turnaround and strong event demand when the value is right. If you find these updates useful, please consider subscribing so you never miss an issue. Thanks for listening and we'll talk soon.

structure.rationale: The episode has a clear, deliberate throughline with distinct segments — consumer sentiment, Target, tariffs, Amazon — each introduced and closed cleanly before moving on.

relevance.rationale: The episode covers timely industry-specific economic and trade developments but sits firmly in trade/business news rather than the News/Politics genre a general listener would expect.

clipability.rationale: Several self-contained, quotable moments exist — the 'resilience has to be activated' framing, the tariff catch explanation, and the AI conversion stat — though most require at least minimal industry context to land fully.

card.tagline: Furniture tariff refunds are real — but the clock is already running out
card.description: A sharp breakdown of why furniture retailers can't follow Walmart's tariff-cut playbook, plus what Amazon's record summer event reveals about cautious consumer spending.
card.reason: Because this episode unpacks a live trade deadline with direct business consequences

Over the last decade, Malaysia's Durian exports to China in particular have soared, driven mainly by prized varieties like the Muzang King, a buttery bittersweet fruit largely grown in Rob that the Chinese have dubbed the hermes of Durians. transcript.scoringWindow: For days now, a long line has been snaking around the two blocks leading up to the Durian ninja fruit stall in Tampanese, a busy township in the east of Singapore. They are waiting patiently for the free fruit the cellar has been giving away daily since mid June, two durians per customer, about six hundred kilograms a day. This generosity is being fueled by an oversupply of Durian across the border in Malaysia, which typically produces about five hundred fifty thousand tons a year, but an unusually abundant twenty twenty six has crashed the price of the prickly pungent fruit beloved in large parts of Asia. Sixty nine year old Churn, who is in the line in Singapore, tells the BBC that he has been having Durian almost every day for him and his family. This is a rare chance to devour Durians of good quality and sometimes at nearly half the price of previous seasons. Customers in Malaysia and neighboring Singapore are swarming fruit stalls where Durians are being sold at huge discounts, or, if they are lucky, given away for free. But while Durian lover's feast, Malaysian farmers are chagrined. They say this glut, or what many have dubbed a Durian tsunami, is the result of a decade long boom during which Malaysians flock to Durian farming to cash in on growing Chinese demand. Back then, a lot of people cut down their rubber trees or oil palms to grow Durians. A lot of the trees planted then are now starting to bear fruit, says lu Yua Thing, the owner of several Durian farms near the Malaysian town of Rob. Over the last decade, Malaysia's Durian exports to China in particular have soared, driven mainly by prized varieties like the Muzang King, a buttery bittersweet fruit largely grown in Rob that the Chinese have dubbed the hermes of Durians. As Muzangking gained popularity, a lot of people got into Muzanking farming, says Lewa Chong, who runs a luxury resort in Durian farm in Malacca. The trees in the newer farms have reached maturity around the same time, result bolting in a glut that has depressed prices across the Malaysian Durian market and impacted exports. Last December, Lou sold his Muzan King Durians to retailers at an average of thirteen point five zero ring it three dollars and thirty cents. This month, Lou says he can only sell them for half the price. Han says he has slashed prices for his musankings by nearly a third, offering them to customers for fifty ring at per kilogram. The market pressure is too high for me, he says, adding that he is now trying to make up for lost profits with other fruit he grows, such as bananas. Some farmers also say they are competing against lower quality yields. Li says that although young trees are producing Durian, the quality is not consistent. This sentiment is shared by Han sing Kong, a Durian farmer and seller in Malaysia's Jehor State, who says many of the Durians flooding the market at throwaway prices are not qualified for export. They don't have any other way to sell it. He says, the name is still Muzan King, but the quality is not up to standard. Also on the heavily discounted list are premium varieties like black thorn, but the cheapest are the Campung Durians, which are so low on the pecking order that they are yet to be named. But for farmers like lew and Han, who have built their business around specialized varieties, this has been a difficult season. They were already grappling with poor harvests because of unfavorable weather when the glut hit. Different periods of Durian growth have different requirements of the weather, Hans says,

structure.rationale: The episode follows a clear narrative arc from consumer lines in Singapore to Malaysian farmer struggles, with identifiable cause-and-effect throughline, though it ends abruptly mid-sentence.

relevance.rationale: The episode covers an interesting economic story about agricultural oversupply but has no meaningful connection to politics or current news events beyond being a timely feature piece.

clipability.rationale: The 'Hermes of Durians' framing and the farmer quotes about halved prices are colorful and quotable, but most moments require the surrounding supply-chain context to land fully.

card.tagline: A fruit glut is wiping out Malaysian farmers who bet everything on durian
card.description: A decade-long farming boom meets a record harvest, crashing durian prices and leaving Malaysian growers selling prized Musang Kings at half price.
card.reason: Because this episode explains a real commodity crisis unfolding right now in Southeast Asia

relevance.rationale: The episode touches on real news hooks (western wildfires, Epstein files) but treats an unverified social media figure as confirmed fact, undermining any substantive political coverage.

clipability.rationale: Nearly every moment requires the host's framing to make sense, and the core claim depends on unverified premise that would confuse or mislead a cold listener.

scored.description: After many years on national television as a broadcaster for The Shopping Channel, Norm Murray has turned his attention to the people, events, and news from his own backyard – Peel Region. A Mississauga resident, Norm travels to various happenings across the GTA to find out – what IS “The Norm” in Peel? On-air, Norm invites conversation, as he engages in thorough discussion through live interviews. Notable interviews include: performers of The Living Arts Centre, Paramount Fine Foods Centre, and The International Centre – everyday people who entertain the community, specifically those who shy away from the “norm,” subsequently establishing a new “norm.” As he likes to say, “we’ve got a lot to talk about.”

And then he talks about the people look, those of you living up in your $5 million penthouse apartments thinking youre going to get a tax break. Well, youre not. Were coming for you next. Well, you know where he lives? He lives in the Soho area, which is very upscale. And I think its, you know, just off Manhattan. And he lives in one of these high-rise $5 million condos. Is he going to give up his home? I dont know. But this guy is uh some of the things that hes talking about, this rent freeze. Anyway, getting back to that, um, developers are not going to want to develop anything. In fact, this is going to force more and more companies, big companies. Now youre talking about developers and construction companies, material companies, and things like that, that are probably going to leave New York and start a headquarters somewhere else because they dont want to be a part of that. I mean, this rent freeze, nobodys going to be able to make any money. And I just dont see the inventory being there in New York City over the next couple of years when that rent freeze really takes hold. And thats just the start of what he wants to do. But the socialism idea, I really cannot remember a time in my life when this was such a major topic. I mean, I do vaguely remember in the 60s all the hubbub with the communists, you know, that were in our government. And socialism and communism, by the way, go hand in hand. And I do remember that, you know, where they were accusing people in our government, people like J. Edgar Hoover and a lot of senators and congresspeople were accusing various people in this country of being communists. Maybe they were, maybe they werent, but but thats the only thing that I remember since then, since that has stopped, I dont recall any accurate conversations, or any conversations really, about socialism. But my question is, has socialism, because it has been around forever, has socialism worked anywhere around the world? And I guess the answer depends on what is meant by socialism or pure socialism, because the term is used to describe very different economic and political systems. Countries that have what they call full state socialism, thats where the government owns or controls most of the economy, have generally struggled to produce sustained prosperity and innovations. Now, countries that have free market economies layered with extensive social welfare programs have been generally much more successful. And countries that have been more successful, every time you hear people talking about socialism and how it hasnt worked anywhere, people say, well, what about the Nordic countries? What about Sweden? What about Denmark? What about Norway? Finland? Theyre doing just fine there. Well, not necessarily. They are not purely socialist countries. They are free market economies with aspects of social welfare programs. And they have been very, very successful. But when you talk about countries that are based on pure socialism, you know what they are. You know, youve got the Soviet Union. Theyve been dealing with this forever. And it eventually, the whole economy in Russia completely collapsed in 1991. We all remember that. It just completely went away and its never recovered. And this war in Ukraine that theyve been going at now for a few years is not doing their economy any good at all. Russia is hurting right now. And for Putin to stand there and blow his whistle and say, Im still the strong one, well, hes not. In fact, he very easily could be on his way out, either through elections, maybe some kind of an overthrow, or maybe one of his own people just decides, you know what, I cannot take any more of this guy, and hes gonna pull out his service weapon and take him out. I mean, you could see something like that. But you youve also seen this in Cuba. And Cuba, if youve seen the pictures over the years, universal health care and high literacy rates are often cited as achievements, but the country has actually experienced decades of very, very low economic growth, huge shortages of basic goods, including food, and limited, if any, really, political freedoms. You also have that kind of a system in Venezuela, but Venezuela is one of those nations thats not fully socialist. They have some free market aspects to their economy, but theyre still hurting. Their dependence on oil revenues and corruption and international sanctions play important roles there. So its not all good in Venezuela. Weve heard all the stories about Venezuela, but Venezuela is a country that probably could rebound. If they got decent leadership now that Maduros gone, if they got some decent leadership in Venezuela, they can probably still keep some of their socialist ideas as long as they keep their free market economy going. So that would not be a bad thing. Venezuela, theyre going to be interesting to watch over the next several years because they could come out of this really smelling like a rose and showing other countries, certainly not the United States, because socialism has no home here in the United States. Everything that we have built over the last 250 years, which by the way, you know, this Saturday is our 250th birthday as a country, the whole thing has been designed to prevent those things, going back the entire 250 years. But another country that is very, very hard-lined is North Korea, one of the worlds most centrally planned economies, marked by chronic food insecurity, very low living standards, they have very strict political control. Now, when youre talking about, and Im sure that some of you are saying, well, what about China? Okay, now, China, that is a different bird altogether, because China, I guess, would probably be the closest thing to a hybrid model that we have out there. China officially describes its system as socialism, but with Chinese characteristics. And theyve been doing that since about the late 1970s. They have introduced extensive market reforms while retaining state ownership in key sectors. In other words, the government hangs on to the things that they want to hang on to, and of course, their one-party political rule. Now, these reforms have been somewhat successful in lifting hundreds of millions out of poverty, though the country continues to face all kinds of challenges related to inequality, to demographics, debt, and uh massive restrictions based on political freedoms. And I think thats the thing. The core of the socialist platform is one that cannot work here in the United States. And it hasnt worked anywhere. It hasnt worked in Russia, it hasnt worked in North Korea, it hasnt worked in Venezuela. And I dont think its working all that well in China either. Its never worked and it never will. If you are a pure socialist economy. Now, you put a hybrid in there and you start adopting free market economy policies. Maybe you can make some headway, but in the United States, were not about that at all. Were not going to let the government control anything. And thats what these far-left candidates are doing. They dont want you to be able to own your home and the land that your home sits on. How ridiculous is that? You can own the structure that you live in, but the land that it sits on you cant own. And youre probably going to have to pay some kind of a lease in order to keep that land. Well, what if the home is paid off? The mortgage is paid off, yet you still have to pay for the land. See, thats never going to fly here in the United States. And were not going to give government that much control. I think the American electorate has pretty much determined that, even though theres a lot of debate on both sides, theres a lot of craziness on both sides of the aisle. But I just dont think that this country, the American people, are not just going to lay down and let the government take over. And thats what they do in these socialist countries. They control everything. You know, Zoran Mamdani in New York, he Wants free grocery stores. He wants government-run grocery stores. Hes got a plan

structure.rationale: The episode meanders from Bernie Sanders to Cuba to North Korea to New York City rent freezes without clear segmentation or signposting, held together loosely by a single thesis rather than deliberate structure.

relevance.rationale: The episode ties its anti-socialism argument to specific current figures like Zoran Mamdani and the 2028 primary landscape, giving it timely hooks, though the core thesis is a well-worn ideological argument rather than breaking news analysis.

clipability.rationale: Most quotable moments require the surrounding ideological setup to land, and the conversational, stream-of-consciousness delivery makes it hard to isolate a self-contained 30-45 second excerpt that works cold.

card.tagline: Socialist candidates are winning primaries — but has socialism ever actually worked?
card.description: Jonathan Prell runs through the global track record of socialism — from the Soviet collapse to Cuba to China's hybrid model — while targeting New York's Zoran Mamdani and the 2028 primary surge.
card.reason: Because socialist candidates are reshaping Democratic primaries heading into 2028

structure.rationale: The episode follows a clear interview format with identifiable segment breaks, a logical progression from legal basics to enforcement gaps to reform proposals, though pacing is somewhat uneven and transitions rely on repetitive show-ID bumpers.

relevance.rationale: The episode addresses specific recent Austrian animal cruelty cases (Husky Buba, cat Schmotzer) and critiques current law, making it timely and substantive, though it is a niche legal/policy topic rather than top-tier political news.

clipability.rationale: The lawyer's explanation of why judges literally cannot sentence first-time offenders to prison under current law is a self-contained, quotable moment, and the international comparison segment also stands alone reasonably well.

card.tagline: Why no one in Austria goes to prison for torturing animals — explained
card.description: A criminal defense lawyer breaks down the legal loopholes that make Austria's animal cruelty law effectively toothless, citing two shocking recent cases.
card.reason: Because this episode exposes a specific legal failure behind this week's headlines

scored.feedTitle: Off the Record:  Portland Real Estate Insider
scored.author: Joe and Makayla
scored.description: Welcome to Off the Record: Portland Real Estate Insider, your ultimate source for all things real estate in Portland Metro! Join Joe and Makayla, as they take you on a thrilling journey behind the scenes of the bustling Portland Metro real estate market. From jaw-dropping success stories to nail-biting challenges, we spill the beans on it all, without holding back. Get ready to gain insider knowledge, uncover hidden gems, and discover the secrets of the trade that no one else will tell you. With our  expert insights, you'll be hooked from the very first episode. So, whether you're a seasoned investor, a first-time homebuyer, or just curious about the world of real estate, be sure to tune in to Off the Record: Portland Real Estate Insider. Don't miss out - hit that subscribe button now and join us on this thrilling ride!

scored.author: Taylor Johnatakis
scored.description:
Peasants Perspective: A Voice from the Edge of Freedom  


Join Taylor Johnatakis, a self-proclaimed “peasant” turned podcaster, on an unfiltered journey through family, faith, and the fight for American ideals. From the depths of DC Jail—where he recorded during a 14-month sentence tied to January 6—to his triumphant return home after a Trump clemency in 2025, Taylor delivers raw, heartfelt commentary for the common man. Expect a mix of gritty storytelling, reflections on liberty lost and reclaimed, and timeless lessons drawn from his life as a septic designer, father, and reluctant rebel. Whether he’s reading Dr. Seuss to his kids or dissecting the state of the republic, Peasants Perspective is a bold, unpolished call to stay grounded amidst chaos. Subscribe for a front-row seat to a story that’s as real as it gets—no filter, no apologies.

structure.rationale: Conversational interview format with a loose but followable arc from Firestone's backstory to the book to the Trump-Putin parallel, though pacing is uneven and the host's interjections occasionally derail momentum.

relevance.rationale: Directly connects Putin's authoritarian playbook to current Trump-era concerns about democratic erosion, with specific references to Magnitsky sanctions, frozen Russian assets for Ukraine, and Navalny — all live geopolitical issues.

clipability.rationale: The Putin-to-Trump pivot moment — 'if some of you are wondering why I've just switched from talking about Putin to talking about Trump, I'm not talking about Trump' — is a near-perfect standalone clip, and the 'government became the mafia' line also stands alone well.

card.tagline: The lawyer who fought Putin is now warning about America's justice system
card.description: Jamison Firestone — who exposed Kremlin corruption and helped pass Magnitsky laws — draws a direct line from Putin's Russia to Trump's America.
card.reason: Because this episode connects Putin's authoritarian tactics to today's American political moment

structure.rationale: The tertulia format produces a recognizable roundtable throughline with clear thematic progression from SEPI corruption to coalition dynamics to left-wing political entrapment, but transitions are conversational and meandering rather than deliberately signposted.

relevance.rationale: The episode directly addresses live Spanish political scandals — SEPI corruption with 24 implicated officials, Marlaska backing the Guardia Civil director, and potential implication of María Jesús Montero — all framed around the Sánchez government's survival prospects heading into anticipated early elections.

clipability.rationale: Several standalone-worthy moments exist — particularly the 'corrupción estructural' framing, the SEPI-as-economic-arm argument, and the left-wing 'harakiri' metaphor — though most require at least minimal context about Spanish political actors to land fully.

card.tagline: 24 imputados en un día: ¿es la SEPI el mayor escándalo de Sánchez?
card.description: Tertulia analiza cómo la macrocausa de la SEPI, con posible imputación de Montero, revela corrupción estructural y deja a los socios del gobierno atrapados sin salida.
card.reason: Because this episode breaks down Spain's biggest active corruption investigation in real time

transcript.scoringWindow: Welcome to K-Buzz, your daily source for the latest Korean entertainment news. With your hosts bringing you all the hardest updates from the K-pop and K-drama world. Today is June 30th, 2026, and weve got some incredible stories to share with you. Lets dive right in. BTS continues to dominate the global music scene with their album A Re Rang, achieving extraordinary streaming milestones just two months after release. The group has accumulated over 53 billion streams across all their albums in an eight-week period, with A Re Rung alone generating 38 billion streams worldwide. The title track Swim became the first song released this year to surpass 500 million streams on Spotify, while tracks Body to Body and Hooligan each cross 200 million streams. Whats particularly impressive is the geographic diversity of their fanbase, with streaming distributed across Latin America at 27%, Northeast Asia at 17%, North America at 14%, Southeast Asia at 13%, and Europe at 12%, proving their truly global appeal. BTSs ongoing A Ray Wrong World Tour is making history as it approaches an unprecedented 1 trillion won in revenue, becoming the first Asian act to reach this milestone. Industry reports indicate the tour has already generated $204 million from just 24 shows through May, averaging an impressive $8.5 million per concert. With 88 total shows scheduled through early next year and 64 performances remaining, conservative estimates project total earnings of at least $748 million, with some analysts predicting the tour could easily surpass $800 million. The second half of the tour features massive stadium venues including Londons Tottenham Hotspur Stadium and Munichs Allianz Arena, which are expected to drive even higher per show revenues and cement BTSs position as a global entertainment powerhouse. Singer Kim Ho jung was released on parole from Soman Correctional Facility on the morning of June 30th, approximately five months ahead of his original November release date. The singer, who was serving a two and a half year sentence for a 2024 drunk driving incident in Apkajong, where he fled the scene and instructed his manager to falsely turn himself in, was greeted by enthusiastic fans holding purple banners with messages like, We waited for you, lets be happy now, and Son, you worked hard, we love you. While his devoted fan base celebrated his return and recently helped him achieve 5 billion cumulative streams on Melon, earning him a spot in the platforms gold club, public reaction has been notably cold with many criticizing the celebratory atmosphere. Kim Ho jung released a handwritten letter to fans expressing that, rather than feeling freedom, he feels increased responsibility and remorse, pledging to serve out his remaining sentence period with accountability while planning to undergo ankle surgery that was delayed during his incarceration. AOMG has launched its first ever girl crew, Keybits, marking a significant moment for the hip-hop label traditionally known for male artists. The five-member group held their debut showcase on June 30th at Yes24 Wonder Rock Hall, introducing their debut EPOXY underscore gen, which symbolizes oxygen and generation, representing their mission to create a new flow in the music industry. The album features five hip hop based tracks, including title song Ox Why, pre-release single Key Beats, and Suck It Up, with notable contributions from AOMG artists including Lee Bag on the title track and Punch Nello featuring on Sub underscore Zero. The group includes three members from Manettes survival show I Land 2, Sunjuan, Yui, and Amjiwon, who emphasize their growth as all-rounders and their determination to become iconic figures in the industry, with member Yesul expressing hope that key bits like becomes a recognizable term in Korean pop culture. The 52-year-old singer, who has over 20 years of teaching experience, personally proposed opening a music course at the school after learning that the institution, which focuses on vocational training for massage therapist certification, struggles to include arts and culture classes in its regular curriculum. Despite the demanding four-hour round trip commute, from her home in Pyongtek to Seoul, BMK travels weekly to teach students in what is essentially a volunteer capacity as a lecturer rather than a regular faculty member. She expressed her hope that music can provide emotional comfort and help students dream of various possibilities, advocating for arts education to be formally included in the regular curriculum of national special education schools. Tomorrow. By Together member Yon Jun has given fans an exciting preview of his upcoming second mini album, No Labels Part 2, by releasing a highlight clip of the title track Ice Cream. The 20-second video showcases the songs vintage drums and addictive bass guitar riff in a funk rock style, while featuring choreography that Yonjoon personally helped create. The performance includes creative movements that visualize ice cream, melting, and playful gestures matching the lyrics Brainfreeze Full of Headaches, demonstrating his artistic input in the albums overall concept. The track uses ice cream as a metaphor for romantic relationships, and the full album is set to drop on July 10th at 1 p.m., marking another solo venture for the multi-talented idol. Tomorrow Buy Together announced their return to the Japanese music scene with their fifth Japanese single, Setsuna Hanabi, scheduled for release on August 19th, marking their first Japanese comeback in approximately 10 months. The new single will include four tracks, three original new songs, plus an alternate version of the title track, with a premium live event planned in Tokyo on the release date. The group has maintained strong momentum in Japan with their third Japanese full album, Starkissed, achieving double platinum certification for 500,000 copies sold, and their recent releases consistently topping the Oricon charts. Their eighth mini album, The Seventh Year, When the Wind Stopped in the Thorns, recently claimed number one on Oricons weekly combined album ranking, tying the record for most number one albums by any artist, Japanese or international, with a total of 14 chart toppers on the platform. BigHip Music issued an apology after member Cortes caused a flight delay on a Paris to Inchon route on June 26th, expressing sincere regret to passengers who experienced inconvenience on the same flight. The delay was attributed to traffic congestion caused by an accident while Cortes was traveling to the airport after completing Paris Fashion Week schedules leading to late boarding. However, the agency firmly denied other circulating rumors, clarifying that allegations about the group changing clothes on the jet bridge during a previous Japan flight last year were completely false. And claims about habitual late boarding were also inaccurate, noting they completed boarding before the deadline during their May departure from Japan. The group is preparing to launch their first world tour with opening shows scheduled for July 18th and 19th at Inspire Arena in Incheon. Well be back tomorrow with more exciting stories and updates, so make sure to tune in for the next episode of K-Buzz. Until then, stay tuned to All Things Korean Entertainment, and well catch you tomorrow.

transcript.scoringWindow: Top Chicago Bears position battles to watch in twenty twenty six. Number four Punt and kick Returner. Written by Michal Duojack. This article is being read by an automated voice brought to you by Everlett. The fields at Hollis Hall and Lake Forest are quiet this week, but in about a month, the Chicago Bears headquarters will be buzzing at the start of training camp in anticipation of a new season. There will be plenty excitement surrounding the Bears once training camp starts. Fans will be eager to see whether head coach Ben Johnson's team can take another big step next season and reach the playoffs in consecutive seasons for the first time since two thousand and six. Many will also want to keep tabs on quarterback Caleb Williams and his growth, but there will be plenty of roster spots up for grabs once camp does start. Based on last year, Johnson and his coaching staff will run a hard camp where each player will have to prove their role on next season's roster. Shaw Local is ranking the top five position battles to watch during training camp. Here's a look at number four on the list. Check back each day this week for the next installment. Number four Punt and kick return returning players Josh Blackwell, Luther burd And, Rossian Johnson, Britton Brown, departed player Devin DuVernay, newcomers Khalif Raymand Xavian Thomas. Why it's worth watching. Bears general manager Ryan Poles and Johnson decided to start fresh at the return position this offseason. Last year's top returner, Devin DuVernay, signed with the Arizona Cardinals during free agency, and polls broad in return candidates. Both in free agency and the draft. DuVernay provided a boost in the Bears return game at times. Last year. He finished fifth in the NFL with inwenty sixty nine kickoff return yards and sixteenth with two hundred and thirty one punt return yards. DuVernay's biggest moment came on the road against the Minnesota Vikings when he returned a kickoff in the final minute that allowed the Bears to pull off one of their late game comeback wins. Poles brought in a veteran and a rookie to fight for the team's return jobs. Chicago signed Cliff Raymond to a one year deal in free agency and then selected Zavian Thomas in the third round of this year's draft. Raymond was an interesting option to bring in this offseason. Not only did have experience with Johnson during their time in Detroit together, but he also proved that he can be dangerous as a punt returner. He finished fourteenth in the NFL last season with two hundred and forty one punt return yards, a year after he led the league with four hundred and thirteen. Raymond has three punt return touchdowns. That first step quickness that he has is unbelievable, and just his aggressiveness bare. Special teams coordinator Richard Hightower said this spring he's an aggressive returner. He wants to return the ball. He wants the ball in his hand. And the other thing is just how well he gets along with his teammates and gets those guys to believe in blocking for him. That has jumped off and out to me in meetings. Thomas will likely make his impact on the Bears roster in the return game. With a loaded wide receiver room. During his four years in college, Thomas proved that he could be electric with the ball in his hands. He returned thirty five kickoffs for nine to fifty two yards and a touchdown. He led the SEC in kickoff return yards as a junior with six thirty three. Thomas also returned sixty punts for five hundred and eighty four yards and one touchdown. The rest of the returning players will likely be backup roles. Josh Blackwell proved to be a dependable kickoff returner, while it's likely that Luther Burden, a Third won't return much anymore as he takes a big role in the receiver room. It will be interesting to watch how much of a competition there will be for the starting return jobs. It could be as simple as Raymond will be the team's punch returner while Thomas will be the team's kickoff returner, But as Raymond tries to prove that he can still make an impact at thirty one while Thomas tries to find his footing in the NFL, there could be some good competitions at the start of training camp.
